          </script><script id="tealium-script" type="text/javascript" async="" src="https://tags.nba.com/nba-web/prod/utag.js"></script><script type="application/ld+json">{"@context":"https://schema.org/","@type":"Article","@id":"https://www.nba.com/news/hall-of-fame-class-of-2024","headline":"Naismith Basketball Hall of Fame announces 13 inductees for Class of 2024","url":"https://www.nba.com/news/hall-of-fame-class-of-2024","articleBody":"> Update: The Class of 2024 will now be inducted on Oct. 12-13\r\nGLENDALE, ARIZ. – The Naismith Basketball Hall of Fame announced the 13 inductees in the Class of 2024 to be celebrated during this year’s Enshrinement festivities on October 12-13. The Class Announcement was made in Glendale, Ariz., the site of the 2024 NCAA Men’s Final Four, and was televised live on ESPN2.\r\n\r\n\r\n\r\nNaismith Basketball Hall Of Fame Class Of 2024\r\nNorth American Committee (in alphabetical order): Chauncey Billups, Vince Carter, Michael Cooper, Walter Davis, Bo Ryan, Charles Smith \r\nWomen's Committee: Seimone Augustus \r\nMen’s Veteran Committee: Dick Barnett\r\nWomen's Veteran Committee: Harley Redin\r\nInternational Committee: Michele Timms\r\nContributors Committee: Doug Collins, Herb Simon, Jerry West\r\n\"Without a shadow of doubt, the accomplishments of the 2024 class stand as a testament to unparalleled excellence,\" said John L. Doleva, President and CEO of the Naismith Basketball Hall of Fame. \"From showcasing unrivaled prowess on the courts and sidelines, spanning the high school, collegiate and professional ranks, to steering the course of basketball across an entire continent and to orchestrating a pair of dynasties, we are honored to pay tribute to these remarkable individuals alongside our esteemed award recipients.”\r\nThe Class of 2024 will be enshrined during festivities in Springfield, Mass., the Birthplace of Basketball, as well as the Mohegan Sun in Uncasville, Conn., on October 12-13. VIP packages and single-event tickets to the 2024 Hall of Fame Enshrinement Weekend are now available at hoophall.com/events/enshrinement-2024.\r\nhttps://twitter.com/Hoophall/status/1776644736604053944\r\n\r\n\r\nNorth American Committee Inductees\r\nCHAUNCEY BILLUPS [Player] – Nicknamed \"Mr. Big Shot\" for his ability to deliver crucial plays in pivotal moments, Billups was a five-time NBA All-Star and the 2004 Finals MVP after leading the Detroit Pistons to their third NBA championship. During his 17-year career, Billups’ averaged 15.2 points and 5.4 assists per game, while his  89.4  free throw percentage is sixth best in NBA/ABA history. Billups was drafted third overall in 1997 by the Boston Celtics after earning Second Team All-American honors at Colorado. He is the current head coach of the Portland Trail Blazers.\r\nVINCE CARTER [Player] – Renowned for his high-flying dunks, the former North Carolina standout was an eight-time All-Star in his 22 NBA seasons (1998-2020). Carter, who won the 2000 Slam Dunk Contest, was named the 1998-99 Rookie of the Year and is the only player in NBA history to play in four different decades. Carter averaged 16.7 points per game (21.3 as a starter) and also played a pivotal role in the success of the 2000 Sydney Olympics, clinching a gold medal with the U.S. Men's Basketball Team. He currently holds the single-season franchise scoring record for the New Jersey/Brooklyn Nets and Toronto Raptors.\r\nMICHAEL COOPER [Player] – Cooper was named to eight NBA All-Defensive Teams, taking home First-Team accolades five times and winning Defensive Player of the Year honors in 1987. The former third-round draft pick (60th overall) out of the University of New Mexico spent his entire 12-year NBA career (1978-90) with the Los Angeles Lakers, winning five championships while routinely matching up with the opponent’s top shooter. Following his playing career, Cooper entered the coaching ranks and earned WNBA Coach of the Year accolades in 2000 with the Los Angeles Sparks before winning back-to-back WNBA titles in 2001-02.\r\nWALTER DAVIS [Player] – The all-time leading scorer in Phoenix Suns’ history was a six-time All-Star (1978-81, ‘84, ‘87) during his 15-year NBA career with the Suns (1977-88), Denver Nuggets (1988-92) and Portland Trail Blazers (1991). Before Phoenix chose Davis with the fifth pick in the 1977 draft, the standout player from North Carolina won a gold medal in the 1976 Summer Olympics. He then averaged 24.2 points per game in his first NBA season and was named the 1978 Rookie of the Year. The late Davis had his No. 6 retired by the Suns, and in 2004, he was enshrined in the team's Ring of Honor.\r\nBO RYAN [Coach] – Ryan went 747-233 (.762) in 32 seasons as a collegiate head coach with the University of Wisconsin-Platteville (1984-89), University of Wisconsin-Milwaukee (1999-2001) and University of Wisconsin (2001-15), including a 364-130 (.737) ledger in 14-plus seasons in Madison. Ryan, honored four times as the Big Ten Coach of the Year, led Wisconsin to unprecedented success, clinching four Big Ten regular-season titles, winning three Big Ten tournament championships, and making back-to-back Final Four appearances (2014-15). The four-time Division III champion (1991, ‘95, ‘98-99) was inducted into the College Basketball Hall of Fame in 2017.\r\nCHARLES SMITH [Coach] – Smith is Louisiana's all-time winningest high school head coach, surpassing the previous mark of 1,071 in 2020. With nine state championships to his name, Smith’s coaching tenure at Peabody Magnet High School began in 1985 after starting as a math teacher in 1975. He guided the Warhorses to a pair of perfect seasons, going 41-0 in 2004 and 2010 and earning national top-five rankings. Smith, ESPN’s National Coach of the Year in 2010, was inducted into the Louisiana Sports Hall of Fame in 2019 and the National High School Athletic Coaches Association Hall of Fame in 2021. In addition, Smith served as head coach at the McDonald’s All-American game in 2020.\r\n\r\n\r\n\r\nWomen’s Committee Inductee\r\nSEIMONE AUGUSTUS [Player] – Augustus, who was selected first overall in the 2006 WNBA Draft, was a four-time champion and eight-time All-Star during her 15-year WNBA career (2006-20). The cornerstone player for the Minnesota Lynx, where she spent 14 of her 15 seasons, averaged 15.4 points per game and was named the 2006 Rookie of the Year and 2011 Finals MVP. At LSU, Augustus led the Tigers to three straight Final Four appearances and won the Naismith College Player of the Year and Wooden Awards in back-to-back campaigns (2005-06).\r\n\r\n\r\n\r\nMen’s Veteran Committee Inductee\r\nDICK BARNETT [Player] – Barnett first gained prominence at Tennessee A&I University, where he led the Tigers to three straight NAIA national championships (1957-59), the first historically black school to claim a men’s basketball title. The “Skull” played 14 NBA seasons with the Syracuse Nationals (1959-61), Los Angeles Lakers (1962-65) and New York Knicks (1965-73), winning a pair of titles in the Big Apple (1970, ‘73) and making his lone All-Star team in 1968. The Knicks retired his No. 12, and the Naismith Basketball Hall of Fame honored his 1957–59 Tigers squad in 2019.\r\n\r\n\r\n\r\nWomen’s Veteran Committee Inductee\r\nHARLEY REDIN [Coach] – Redin went 431-66 in 18 seasons leading Wayland Baptist’s women’s basketball program and captured six AAU National Championships. He coached the Flying Queens to two undefeated women’s seasons (1956, ‘57), 17 top-five finishes, and went 110-2 during his first four campaigns at Wayland Baptist. The late Redin also coached the Women’s U.S. National Team in 1959, the 1971 Pan-American Games, and the 1963 World Tournament in Peru. He was the recipient of the Jostens-Berenson Service Award by the Women’s Basketball Coaches Association for his lifetime of service to women’s basketball in 1992, inducted into the Women’s Basketball Hall of Fame in 1999, and honored with the Naismith Award for Outstanding Contribution to the game of women’s basketball in 2000.\r\n\r\n\r\n\r\nInternational Committee Inductee\r\nMICHELE TIMMS [Player] – A trailblazer in Australian women’s basketball, Timms became well-known as one of the best point guards in the world, representing the Australian national team throughout the 1980s and 1990s. Timms won a pair of Olympic medals with the Opals, winning bronze in 1996 and silver in 2000, and was the first Australian, male or female, to play professional basketball internationally when she suited up for Lotus München in Germany. In addition, Timms spent five seasons with the Phoenix Mercury, guiding the team to a berth in the 1998 WNBA Finals and earning an All-Star selection in 1999. The Sport Australia Hall of Fame inducted her in 2003, the Women's Basketball Hall of Fame in 2008, and the FIBA Hall of Fame in 2016. The Mercury retired her No. 7 jersey in 2002.\r\n\r\n\r\n\r\nContributor Committee Inductees\r\nDOUG COLLINS – Collins' versatile career in basketball is marked by significant achievements in roles ranging from player to coach to broadcaster, underscoring his wide-ranging impact on the sport. Following an NBA career where he was a four-time All-Star with the Philadelphia 76ers, he transitioned into coaching and accumulated over 400 wins with the Chicago Bulls (1986-89), Detroit Pistons (1995-98), Washington Wizards (2001-03) and 76ers (2010-13). Collins, who coached Hall of Famer Michael Jordan in both Chicago and Washington, also served as an analyst for various NBA-related broadcasts for CBS, NBC, TNT, TBS, and ABC/ESPN, along with working for NBC at the 2008 and 2012 Summer Olympics.\r\nHERB SIMON – Simon, the longest-tenured governor in the history of the NBA, purchased the Indiana Pacers, along with his late brother Mel, back in 1983. Under Simon’s stewardship, the Pacers won numerous Central Division titles and made the franchise’s first NBA Finals in 2000. Throughout his ownership period, the Pacers have been home to legendary players like Jermaine O’Neal, Rik Smits, and Reggie Miller (Class of 2012). Beyond ownership, Simon's influence extends deeply into the heart of the Pacers’ organization, shaping its identity and impact both on and off the court through his philanthropic endeavors and civic engagement.\r\nJERRY WEST – Previously enshrined as a player and as a member of the 1960 U.S. Olympic Team, West then went on to establish himself off the court as one of the most successful executives in the sport’s history. West played a pivotal role as the architect of a pair of Los Angeles Lakers’ dynasties during the 1980s and 2000s, drafting key players like Magic Johnson and James Worthy, acquiring Kobe Bryant, signing free agent Shaquille O'Neal, and hiring head coach Phil Jackson. He totaled eight NBA championships in Los Angeles (1980, ‘82, ‘85, ‘87-88, 2000-02) and was named Executive of the Year twice (1995, 2004). Following his time in the Lakers’ front office (1979-2000), West served as GM of the Memphis Grizzlies from 2002-07 before earning two additional NBA championships as an executive with the Golden State Warriors (2015, ‘17). West is the first member of the Naismith Basketball Hall of Fame to be inducted as both a player and a contributor.","dateCreated":"2024-04-06T16:27:21Z","description":"The Class of 2024 will be enshrined during festivities on October 12-13.","thumbnailUrl":"https://cdn.nba.com/manage/2020/02/GettyImages-3061387-scaled-e1603219698667.jpg","articleSection":"Hall of Fame: Class of class="ArticleHeader_ah__N3ONy"><div class="ArticleHeader_ahBlock__sa_pW"><div class="ArticleHeader_ahCategory__UVU9k"><h3 class="ArticleHeader_ahCattext__YHYzU">Hall of Fame: Class of 2024</h3><div class="DisplayAd_adTransitioner__G5t4A"><div class="GoogleDisplayAd_ad__Oum07" id="article_ad_s_1" data-ad-type="sponsor"></div></div></div><h1 class="ArticleHeader_ahTitle__qRVms">Naismith Basketball Hall of Fame announces 13 inductees for Class of 2024</h1><p class="ArticleHeader_ahSubtitle__jGmAK">The Class of 2024 will be enshrined during festivities on October 12-13.</p></div><div class="ArticleHeader_ahBy__mKzod"><div class="ArticleHeader_ahAuthor__IteTb"><div class="ArticleAuthor_base__KkZ3k"><div class="ArticleAuthor_bio__ClsuO"><div><p class="ArticleAuthor_authorName__oeNmP">Official release</p></div><div class="ArticleAuthor_authorSocial__pENOl"></div></div></div><time class="ArticleHeader_ahDate__sR9IG">April 6, 2024 6:27 PM</time></div></div></div><div class="Columns_col__b1RWg"><div class="Columns_left__7mcek"><div class="ArticleContent_article__bAlgR"><div id="attachment_1183792" style="width: 1578px" class="wp-caption alignnone"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" aria-describedby="caption-attachment-1183792" class="wp-image-1183792 size-large" src="https://cdn.nba.com/manage/2023/09/GettyImages-167755410-1568x882.jpg" alt="" width="1080" height="607" srcset="https://cdn.nba.com/manage/2023/09/GettyImages-167755410-1568x882.jpg 1568w, https://cdn.nba.com/manage/2023/09/GettyImages-167755410-784x441.jpg 784w, https://cdn.nba.com/manage/2023/09/GettyImages-167755410-768x432.jpg 768w, https://cdn.nba.com/manage/2023/09/GettyImages-167755410-1536x864.jpg 1536w, https://cdn.nba.com/manage/2023/09/GettyImages-167755410.jpg 1920w" sizes="(max-width: 1568px) 100vw, 1568px"><p id="caption-attachment-1183792" class="wp-caption-text">Vince Carter is among the 13 new members inducted into the Naismith Basketball Hall of Fame for 2024.</p></div>
<p><strong>> Update: <a href="https://www.nba.com/news/naismith-basketball-hall-of-fame-moves-enshrinement-dates-to-oct-12-13">The Class of 2024 will now be inducted on Oct. 12-13</a></strong></p>
<p style="font-weight: 400;"><strong>GLENDALE, ARIZ.</strong> – The Naismith Basketball Hall of Fame announced the 13 inductees in the Class of 2024 to be celebrated during this year’s Enshrinement festivities on October 12-13. The Class Announcement was made in Glendale, Ariz., the site of the 2024 NCAA Men’s Final Four, and was televised live on ESPN2.</p>
<hr>
<p style="font-weight: 400;"><span style="font-size: 18pt;"><strong>Naismith Basketball Hall Of Fame Class Of 2024</strong></span></p>
<p style="font-weight: 400;"><strong>North American Committee (in alphabetical order):</strong> Chauncey Billups, Vince Carter, Michael Cooper, Walter Davis, Bo Ryan, Charles Smith<strong> </strong></p>
<p style="font-weight: 400;"><strong>Women’s Committee: </strong>Seimone Augustus<strong> </strong></p>
<p style="font-weight: 400;"><strong>Men’s Veteran Committee: </strong>Dick Barnett</p>
<p style="font-weight: 400;"><strong>Women’s Veteran Committee:</strong> Harley Redin</p>
<p style="font-weight: 400;"><strong>International Committee:</strong> Michele Timms</p>
<p style="font-weight: 400;"><strong>Contributors Committee: </strong>Doug Collins, Herb Simon, Jerry West</p>
<p style="font-weight: 400;">“Without a shadow of doubt, the accomplishments of the 2024 class stand as a testament to unparalleled excellence,” said John L. Doleva, President and CEO of the Naismith Basketball Hall of Fame. “From showcasing unrivaled prowess on the courts and sidelines, spanning the high school, collegiate and professional ranks, to steering the course of basketball across an entire continent and to orchestrating a pair of dynasties, we are honored to pay tribute to these remarkable individuals alongside our esteemed award recipients.”</p>
<p style="font-weight: 400;">The Class of 2024 will be enshrined during festivities in Springfield, Mass., the Birthplace of Basketball, as well as the Mohegan Sun in Uncasville, Conn., on October 12-13. VIP packages and single-event tickets to the 2024 Hall of Fame Enshrinement Weekend are now available at <a href="https://urldefense.com/v3/__https://www.hoophall.com/events/enshrinement-2024__;!!AQdq3sQhfUj4q8uUguY!hQEIyv9hPQ_7kN3gHjxxlazyQV2v6BjwHeMA2u7dA4TXFkdFzy0DW40fTsWkZa8hms2LHBK4RynGS4z4q-M$" data-outlook-id="7101ac7e-49e3-47a2-b4e6-3c5db76f3285">hoophall.com/events/enshrinement-2024</a>.</p>
<blockquote class="twitter-tweet" data-width="500" data-dnt="true">
<p lang="en" dir="ltr">Officially official. 🤩🏆🏀 <a href="https://twitter.com/hashtag/24HoopClass?src=hash&ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">#24HoopClass</a> <a href="https://t.co/lfYFONUNR7">pic.twitter.com/lfYFONUNR7</a></p>
<p>— Basketball HOF (@Hoophall) <a href="https://twitter.com/Hoophall/status/1776644736604053944?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">April 6, 2024</a></p></blockquote>
<p><script async="" src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script></p>
<hr>
<p style="font-weight: 400;"><span style="font-size: 18pt;"><strong>North American Committee Inductees</strong></span></p>
<p style="font-weight: 400;"><strong>CHAUNCEY BILLUPS </strong>[Player] – Nicknamed “Mr. Big Shot” for his ability to deliver crucial plays in pivotal moments, Billups was a five-time NBA All-Star and the 2004 Finals MVP after leading the Detroit Pistons to their third NBA championship. During his 17-year career, Billups’ averaged 15.2 points and 5.4 assists per game, while his  89.4  free throw percentage is sixth best in NBA/ABA history. Billups was drafted third overall in 1997 by the Boston Celtics after earning Second Team All-American honors at Colorado. He is the current head coach of the Portland Trail Blazers.</p>
<p style="font-weight: 400;"><strong>VINCE CARTER </strong>[Player] – Renowned for his high-flying dunks, the former North Carolina standout was an eight-time All-Star in his 22 NBA seasons (1998-2020). Carter, who won the 2000 Slam Dunk Contest, was named the 1998-99 Rookie of the Year and is the only player in NBA history to play in four different decades. Carter averaged 16.7 points per game (21.3 as a starter) and also played a pivotal role in the success of the 2000 Sydney Olympics, clinching a gold medal with the U.S. Men’s Basketball Team. He currently holds the single-season franchise scoring record for the New Jersey/Brooklyn Nets and Toronto Raptors.</p>
<p style="font-weight: 400;"><strong>MICHAEL COOPER </strong>[Player] – Cooper was named to eight NBA All-Defensive Teams, taking home First-Team accolades five times and winning Defensive Player of the Year honors in 1987. The former third-round draft pick (60th overall) out of the University of New Mexico spent his entire 12-year NBA career (1978-90) with the Los Angeles Lakers, winning five championships while routinely matching up with the opponent’s top shooter. Following his playing career, Cooper entered the coaching ranks and earned WNBA Coach of the Year accolades in 2000 with the Los Angeles Sparks before winning back-to-back WNBA titles in 2001-02.</p>
<p style="font-weight: 400;"><strong>WALTER DAVIS</strong> [Player] – The all-time leading scorer in Phoenix Suns’ history was a six-time All-Star (1978-81, ‘84, ‘87) during his 15-year NBA career with the Suns (1977-88), Denver Nuggets (1988-92) and Portland Trail Blazers (1991). Before Phoenix chose Davis with the fifth pick in the 1977 draft, the standout player from North Carolina won a gold medal in the 1976 Summer Olympics. He then averaged 24.2 points per game in his first NBA season and was named the 1978 Rookie of the Year. The late Davis had his No. 6 retired by the Suns, and in 2004, he was enshrined in the team’s Ring of Honor.</p>
<p style="font-weight: 400;"><strong>BO RYAN </strong>[Coach] – Ryan went 747-233 (.762) in 32 seasons as a collegiate head coach with the University of Wisconsin-Platteville (1984-89), University of Wisconsin-Milwaukee (1999-2001) and University of Wisconsin (2001-15), including a 364-130 (.737) ledger in 14-plus seasons in Madison. Ryan, honored four times as the Big Ten Coach of the Year, led Wisconsin to unprecedented success, clinching four Big Ten regular-season titles, winning three Big Ten tournament championships, and making back-to-back Final Four appearances (2014-15). The four-time Division III champion (1991, ‘95, ‘98-99) was inducted into the College Basketball Hall of Fame in 2017.</p>
<p style="font-weight: 400;"><strong>CHARLES SMITH</strong> [Coach] – Smith is Louisiana’s all-time winningest high school head coach, surpassing the previous mark of 1,071 in 2020. With nine state championships to his name, Smith’s coaching tenure at Peabody Magnet High School began in 1985 after starting as a math teacher in 1975. He guided the Warhorses to a pair of perfect seasons, going 41-0 in 2004 and 2010 and earning national top-five rankings. Smith, ESPN’s National Coach of the Year in 2010, was inducted into the Louisiana Sports Hall of Fame in 2019 and the National High School Athletic Coaches Association Hall of Fame in 2021. In addition, Smith served as head coach at the McDonald’s All-American game in 2020.</p>
<hr>
<p style="font-weight: 400;"><span style="font-size: 18pt;"><strong>Women’s Committee Inductee</strong></span></p>
<p style="font-weight: 400;"><strong>SEIMONE AUGUSTUS </strong>[Player] – Augustus, who was selected first overall in the 2006 WNBA Draft, was a four-time champion and eight-time All-Star during her 15-year WNBA career (2006-20). The cornerstone player for the Minnesota Lynx, where she spent 14 of her 15 seasons, averaged 15.4 points per game and was named the 2006 Rookie of the Year and 2011 Finals MVP. At LSU, Augustus led the Tigers to three straight Final Four appearances and won the Naismith College Player of the Year and Wooden Awards in back-to-back campaigns (2005-06).</p>
<hr>
<p style="font-weight: 400;"><span style="font-size: 18pt;"><strong>Men’s Veteran Committee Inductee</strong></span></p>
<p style="font-weight: 400;"><strong>DICK BARNETT </strong>[Player] – Barnett first gained prominence at Tennessee A&I University, where he led the Tigers to three straight NAIA national championships (1957-59), the first historically black school to claim a men’s basketball title. The “Skull” played 14 NBA seasons with the Syracuse Nationals (1959-61), Los Angeles Lakers (1962-65) and New York Knicks (1965-73), winning a pair of titles in the Big Apple (1970, ‘73) and making his lone All-Star team in 1968. The Knicks retired his No. 12, and the Naismith Basketball Hall of Fame honored his 1957–59 Tigers squad in 2019.</p>
<hr>
<p style="font-weight: 400;"><span style="font-size: 18pt;"><strong>Women’s Veteran Committee Inductee</strong></span></p>
<p style="font-weight: 400;"><strong>HARLEY REDIN</strong> [Coach] – Redin went 431-66 in 18 seasons leading Wayland Baptist’s women’s basketball program and captured six AAU National Championships. He coached the Flying Queens to two undefeated women’s seasons (1956, ‘57), 17 top-five finishes, and went 110-2 during his first four campaigns at Wayland Baptist. The late Redin also coached the Women’s U.S. National Team in 1959, the 1971 Pan-American Games, and the 1963 World Tournament in Peru. He was the recipient of the Jostens-Berenson Service Award by the Women’s Basketball Coaches Association for his lifetime of service to women’s basketball in 1992, inducted into the Women’s Basketball Hall of Fame in 1999, and honored with the Naismith Award for Outstanding Contribution to the game of women’s basketball in 2000.</p>
<hr>
<p style="font-weight: 400;"><span style="font-size: 18pt;"><strong>International Committee Inductee</strong></span></p>
<p style="font-weight: 400;"><strong>MICHELE TIMMS </strong>[Player] – A trailblazer in Australian women’s basketball, Timms became well-known as one of the best point guards in the world, representing the Australian national team throughout the 1980s and 1990s. Timms won a pair of Olympic medals with the Opals, winning bronze in 1996 and silver in 2000, and was the first Australian, male or female, to play professional basketball internationally when she suited up for Lotus München in Germany. In addition, Timms spent five seasons with the Phoenix Mercury, guiding the team to a berth in the 1998 WNBA Finals and earning an All-Star selection in 1999. The Sport Australia Hall of Fame inducted her in 2003, the Women’s Basketball Hall of Fame in 2008, and the FIBA Hall of Fame in 2016. The Mercury retired her No. 7 jersey in 2002.</p>
<hr>
<p style="font-weight: 400;"><span style="font-size: 18pt;"><strong>Contributor Committee Inductees</strong></span></p>
<p style="font-weight: 400;"><strong>DOUG COLLINS </strong>– Collins’ versatile career in basketball is marked by significant achievements in roles ranging from player to coach to broadcaster, underscoring his wide-ranging impact on the sport. Following an NBA career where he was a four-time All-Star with the Philadelphia 76ers, he transitioned into coaching and accumulated over 400 wins with the Chicago Bulls (1986-89), Detroit Pistons (1995-98), Washington Wizards (2001-03) and 76ers (2010-13). Collins, who coached Hall of Famer Michael Jordan in both Chicago and Washington, also served as an analyst for various NBA-related broadcasts for CBS, NBC, TNT, TBS, and ABC/ESPN, along with working for NBC at the 2008 and 2012 Summer Olympics.</p>
<p style="font-weight: 400;"><strong>HERB SIMON</strong> – Simon, the longest-tenured governor in the history of the NBA, purchased the Indiana Pacers, along with his late brother Mel, back in 1983. Under Simon’s stewardship, the Pacers won numerous Central Division titles and made the franchise’s first NBA Finals in 2000. Throughout his ownership period, the Pacers have been home to legendary players like Jermaine O’Neal, Rik Smits, and Reggie Miller (Class of 2012). Beyond ownership, Simon’s influence extends deeply into the heart of the Pacers’ organization, shaping its identity and impact both on and off the court through his philanthropic endeavors and civic engagement.</p>
<p style="font-weight: 400;"><strong>JERRY WEST</strong> – Previously enshrined as a player and as a member of the 1960 U.S. Olympic Team, West then went on to establish himself off the court as one of the most successful executives in the sport’s history. West played a pivotal role as the architect of a pair of Los Angeles Lakers’ dynasties during the 1980s and 2000s, drafting key players like Magic Johnson and James Worthy, acquiring Kobe Bryant, signing free agent Shaquille O’Neal, and hiring head coach Phil Jackson. He totaled eight NBA championships in Los Angeles (1980, ‘82, ‘85, ‘87-88, 2000-02) and was named Executive of the Year twice (1995, 2004). Following his time in the Lakers’ front office (1979-2000), West served as GM of the Memphis Grizzlies from 2002-07 before earning two additional NBA championships as an executive with the Golden State Warriors (2015, ‘17). <strong><u>West is the first member of the Naismith Basketball Hall of Fame to be inducted as both a player and a contributor.</u></strong></p>

release","firstName":"","lastName":"","organization":"","isNBAStaff":false,"authorPhoto":"","socialHandle":"","socialLink":"","wpseoTitle":"","wpseoMetadesc":"","description":"","userUrl":"","featuredAuthor":false},"contentText":"\u0026gt; Update: The Class of 2024 will now be inducted on Oct. 12-13\r\nGLENDALE, ARIZ. – The Naismith Basketball Hall of Fame announced the 13 inductees in the Class of 2024 to be celebrated during this year’s Enshrinement festivities on October 12-13. The Class Announcement was made in Glendale, Ariz., the site of the 2024 NCAA Men’s Final Four, and was televised live on ESPN2.\r\n\r\n\r\n\r\nNaismith Basketball Hall Of Fame Class Of 2024\r\nNorth American Committee (in alphabetical order): Chauncey Billups, Vince Carter, Michael Cooper, Walter Davis, Bo Ryan, Charles Smith \r\nWomen's Committee: Seimone Augustus \r\nMen’s Veteran Committee: Dick Barnett\r\nWomen's Veteran Committee: Harley Redin\r\nInternational Committee: Michele Timms\r\nContributors Committee: Doug Collins, Herb Simon, Jerry West\r\n\"Without a shadow of doubt, the accomplishments of the 2024 class stand as a testament to unparalleled excellence,\" said John L. Doleva, President and CEO of the Naismith Basketball Hall of Fame. \"From showcasing unrivaled prowess on the courts and sidelines, spanning the high school, collegiate and professional ranks, to steering the course of basketball across an entire continent and to orchestrating a pair of dynasties, we are honored to pay tribute to these remarkable individuals alongside our esteemed award recipients.”\r\nThe Class of 2024 will be enshrined during festivities in Springfield, Mass., the Birthplace of Basketball, as well as the Mohegan Sun in Uncasville, Conn., on October 12-13. VIP packages and single-event tickets to the 2024 Hall of Fame Enshrinement Weekend are now available at hoophall.com/events/enshrinement-2024.\r\nhttps://twitter.com/Hoophall/status/1776644736604053944\r\n\r\n\r\nNorth American Committee Inductees\r\nCHAUNCEY BILLUPS [Player] – Nicknamed \"Mr. Big Shot\" for his ability to deliver crucial plays in pivotal moments, Billups was a five-time NBA All-Star and the 2004 Finals MVP after leading the Detroit Pistons to their third NBA championship. During his 17-year career, Billups’ averaged 15.2 points and 5.4 assists per game, while his  89.4  free throw percentage is sixth best in NBA/ABA history. Billups was drafted third overall in 1997 by the Boston Celtics after earning Second Team All-American honors at Colorado. He is the current head coach of the Portland Trail Blazers.\r\nVINCE CARTER [Player] – Renowned for his high-flying dunks, the former North Carolina standout was an eight-time All-Star in his 22 NBA seasons (1998-2020). Carter, who won the 2000 Slam Dunk Contest, was named the 1998-99 Rookie of the Year and is the only player in NBA history to play in four different decades. Carter averaged 16.7 points per game (21.3 as a starter) and also played a pivotal role in the success of the 2000 Sydney Olympics, clinching a gold medal with the U.S. Men's Basketball Team. He currently holds the single-season franchise scoring record for the New Jersey/Brooklyn Nets and Toronto Raptors.\r\nMICHAEL COOPER [Player] – Cooper was named to eight NBA All-Defensive Teams, taking home First-Team accolades five times and winning Defensive Player of the Year honors in 1987. The former third-round draft pick (60th overall) out of the University of New Mexico spent his entire 12-year NBA career (1978-90) with the Los Angeles Lakers, winning five championships while routinely matching up with the opponent’s top shooter. Following his playing career, Cooper entered the coaching ranks and earned WNBA Coach of the Year accolades in 2000 with the Los Angeles Sparks before winning back-to-back WNBA titles in 2001-02.\r\nWALTER DAVIS [Player] – The all-time leading scorer in Phoenix Suns’ history was a six-time All-Star (1978-81, ‘84, ‘87) during his 15-year NBA career with the Suns (1977-88), Denver Nuggets (1988-92) and Portland Trail Blazers (1991). Before Phoenix chose Davis with the fifth pick in the 1977 draft, the standout player from North Carolina won a gold medal in the 1976 Summer Olympics. He then averaged 24.2 points per game in his first NBA season and was named the 1978 Rookie of the Year. The late Davis had his No. 6 retired by the Suns, and in 2004, he was enshrined in the team's Ring of Honor.\r\nBO RYAN [Coach] – Ryan went 747-233 (.762) in 32 seasons as a collegiate head coach with the University of Wisconsin-Platteville (1984-89), University of Wisconsin-Milwaukee (1999-2001) and University of Wisconsin (2001-15), including a 364-130 (.737) ledger in 14-plus seasons in Madison. Ryan, honored four times as the Big Ten Coach of the Year, led Wisconsin to unprecedented success, clinching four Big Ten regular-season titles, winning three Big Ten tournament championships, and making back-to-back Final Four appearances (2014-15). The four-time Division III champion (1991, ‘95, ‘98-99) was inducted into the College Basketball Hall of Fame in 2017.\r\nCHARLES SMITH [Coach] – Smith is Louisiana's all-time winningest high school head coach, surpassing the previous mark of 1,071 in 2020. With nine state championships to his name, Smith’s coaching tenure at Peabody Magnet High School began in 1985 after starting as a math teacher in 1975. He guided the Warhorses to a pair of perfect seasons, going 41-0 in 2004 and 2010 and earning national top-five rankings. Smith, ESPN’s National Coach of the Year in 2010, was inducted into the Louisiana Sports Hall of Fame in 2019 and the National High School Athletic Coaches Association Hall of Fame in 2021. In addition, Smith served as head coach at the McDonald’s All-American game in 2020.\r\n\r\n\r\n\r\nWomen’s Committee Inductee\r\nSEIMONE AUGUSTUS [Player] – Augustus, who was selected first overall in the 2006 WNBA Draft, was a four-time champion and eight-time All-Star during her 15-year WNBA career (2006-20). The cornerstone player for the Minnesota Lynx, where she spent 14 of her 15 seasons, averaged 15.4 points per game and was named the 2006 Rookie of the Year and 2011 Finals MVP. At LSU, Augustus led the Tigers to three straight Final Four appearances and won the Naismith College Player of the Year and Wooden Awards in back-to-back campaigns (2005-06).\r\n\r\n\r\n\r\nMen’s Veteran Committee Inductee\r\nDICK BARNETT [Player] – Barnett first gained prominence at Tennessee A\u0026amp;I University, where he led the Tigers to three straight NAIA national championships (1957-59), the first historically black school to claim a men’s basketball title. The “Skull” played 14 NBA seasons with the Syracuse Nationals (1959-61), Los Angeles Lakers (1962-65) and New York Knicks (1965-73), winning a pair of titles in the Big Apple (1970, ‘73) and making his lone All-Star team in 1968. The Knicks retired his No. 12, and the Naismith Basketball Hall of Fame honored his 1957–59 Tigers squad in 2019.\r\n\r\n\r\n\r\nWomen’s Veteran Committee Inductee\r\nHARLEY REDIN [Coach] – Redin went 431-66 in 18 seasons leading Wayland Baptist’s women’s basketball program and captured six AAU National Championships. He coached the Flying Queens to two undefeated women’s seasons (1956, ‘57), 17 top-five finishes, and went 110-2 during his first four campaigns at Wayland Baptist. The late Redin also coached the Women’s U.S. National Team in 1959, the 1971 Pan-American Games, and the 1963 World Tournament in Peru. He was the recipient of the Jostens-Berenson Service Award by the Women’s Basketball Coaches Association for his lifetime of service to women’s basketball in 1992, inducted into the Women’s Basketball Hall of Fame in 1999, and honored with the Naismith Award for Outstanding Contribution to the game of women’s basketball in 2000.\r\n\r\n\r\n\r\nInternational Committee Inductee\r\nMICHELE TIMMS [Player] – A trailblazer in Australian women’s basketball, Timms became well-known as one of the best point guards in the world, representing the Australian national team throughout the 1980s and 1990s. Timms won a pair of Olympic medals with the Opals, winning bronze in 1996 and silver in 2000, and was the first Australian, male or female, to play professional basketball internationally when she suited up for Lotus München in Germany. In addition, Timms spent five seasons with the Phoenix Mercury, guiding the team to a berth in the 1998 WNBA Finals and earning an All-Star selection in 1999. The Sport Australia Hall of Fame inducted her in 2003, the Women's Basketball Hall of Fame in 2008, and the FIBA Hall of Fame in 2016. The Mercury retired her No. 7 jersey in 2002.\r\n\r\n\r\n\r\nContributor Committee Inductees\r\nDOUG COLLINS – Collins' versatile career in basketball is marked by significant achievements in roles ranging from player to coach to broadcaster, underscoring his wide-ranging impact on the sport. Following an NBA career where he was a four-time All-Star with the Philadelphia 76ers, he transitioned into coaching and accumulated over 400 wins with the Chicago Bulls (1986-89), Detroit Pistons (1995-98), Washington Wizards (2001-03) and 76ers (2010-13). Collins, who coached Hall of Famer Michael Jordan in both Chicago and Washington, also served as an analyst for various NBA-related broadcasts for CBS, NBC, TNT, TBS, and ABC/ESPN, along with working for NBC at the 2008 and 2012 Summer Olympics.\r\nHERB SIMON – Simon, the longest-tenured governor in the history of the NBA, purchased the Indiana Pacers, along with his late brother Mel, back in 1983. Under Simon’s stewardship, the Pacers won numerous Central Division titles and made the franchise’s first NBA Finals in 2000. Throughout his ownership period, the Pacers have been home to legendary players like Jermaine O’Neal, Rik Smits, and Reggie Miller (Class of 2012). Beyond ownership, Simon's influence extends deeply into the heart of the Pacers’ organization, shaping its identity and impact both on and off the court through his philanthropic endeavors and civic engagement.\r\nJERRY WEST – Previously enshrined as a player and as a member of the 1960 U.S. Olympic Team, West then went on to establish himself off the court as one of the most successful executives in the sport’s history. West played a pivotal role as the architect of a pair of Los Angeles Lakers’ dynasties during the 1980s and 2000s, drafting key players like Magic Johnson and James Worthy, acquiring Kobe Bryant, signing free agent Shaquille O'Neal, and hiring head coach Phil Jackson. He totaled eight NBA championships in Los Angeles (1980, ‘82, ‘85, ‘87-88, 2000-02) and was named Executive of the Year twice (1995, 2004). Following his time in the Lakers’ front office (1979-2000), West served as GM of the Memphis Grizzlies from 2002-07 before earning two additional NBA championships as an executive with the Golden State Warriors (2015, ‘17). West is the first member of the Naismith Basketball Hall of Fame to be inducted as both a player and a contributor.","contentFiltered":"\u003cdiv id=\"attachment_1183792\" style=\"width: 1578px\" class=\"wp-caption alignnone\"\u003e\u003cimg loading=\"lazy\" decoding=\"async\" aria-describedby=\"caption-attachment-1183792\" class=\"wp-image-1183792 size-large\" src=\"https://cdn.nba.com/manage/2023/09/GettyImages-167755410-1568x882.jpg\" alt=\"\" width=\"1568\" height=\"882\" srcset=\"https://cdn.nba.com/manage/2023/09/GettyImages-167755410-1568x882.jpg 1568w, https://cdn.nba.com/manage/2023/09/GettyImages-167755410-784x441.jpg 784w, https://cdn.nba.com/manage/2023/09/GettyImages-167755410-768x432.jpg 768w, https://cdn.nba.com/manage/2023/09/GettyImages-167755410-1536x864.jpg 1536w, https://cdn.nba.com/manage/2023/09/GettyImages-167755410.jpg 1920w\" sizes=\"(max-width: 1568px) 100vw, 1568px\" /\u003e\u003cp id=\"caption-attachment-1183792\" class=\"wp-caption-text\"\u003eVince Carter is among the 13 new members inducted into the Naismith Basketball Hall of Fame for 2024.\u003c/p\u003e\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong\u003e\u0026gt; Update: \u003ca href=\"https://www.nba.com/news/naismith-basketball-hall-of-fame-moves-enshrinement-dates-to-oct-12-13\"\u003eThe Class of 2024 will now be inducted on Oct. 12-13\u003c/a\u003e\u003c/strong\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp style=\"font-weight: 400;\"\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eGLENDALE, ARIZ.\u003c/strong\u003e – The Naismith Basketball Hall of Fame announced the 13 inductees in the Class of 2024 to be celebrated during this year’s Enshrinement festivities on October 12-13. Throughout his ownership period, the Pacers have been home to legendary players like Jermaine O’Neal, Rik Smits, and Reggie Miller (Class of 2012). Beyond ownership, Simon\u0026#8217;s influence extends deeply into the heart of the Pacers’ organization, shaping its identity and impact both on and off the court through his philanthropic endeavors and civic engagement.\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp style=\"font-weight: 400;\"\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eJERRY WEST\u003c/strong\u003e – Previously enshrined as a player and as a member of the 1960 U.S. Olympic Team, West then went on to establish himself off the court as one of the most successful executives in the sport’s history. West played a pivotal role as the architect of a pair of Los Angeles Lakers’ dynasties during the 1980s and 2000s, drafting key players like Magic Johnson and James Worthy, acquiring Kobe Bryant, signing free agent Shaquille O\u0026#8217;Neal, and hiring head coach Phil Jackson. He totaled eight NBA championships in Los Angeles (1980, ‘82, ‘85, ‘87-88, 2000-02) and was named Executive of the Year twice (1995, 2004). Following his time in the Lakers’ front office (1979-2000), West served as GM of the Memphis Grizzlies from 2002-07 before earning two additional NBA championships as an executive with the Golden State Warriors (2015, ‘17). \u003cstrong\u003e\u003cu\u003eWest is the first member of the Naismith Basketball Hall of Fame to be inducted as both a player and a contributor.\u003c/u\u003e\u003c/strong\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n","coverImage":"","coverImageId":"","hideFromPlatform":"false","authorHide":false,"excerpt":"The Class of 2024 will be enshrined during gold_text:
Naismith Basketball Hall of Fame announces 13 inductees for Class of 2024
The Class of 2024 will be enshrined during festivities on October 12-13.
> Update: The Class of 2024 will now be inducted on Oct. 12-13

GLENDALE, ARIZ. – The Naismith Basketball Hall of Fame announced the 13 inductees in the Class of 2024 to be celebrated during this year’s Enshrinement festivities on October 12-13. The Class Announcement was made in Glendale, Ariz., the site of the 2024 NCAA Men’s Final Four, and was televised live on ESPN2.

Naismith Basketball Hall Of Fame Class Of 2024

North American Committee (in alphabetical order): Chauncey Billups, Vince Carter, Michael Cooper, Walter Davis, Bo Ryan, Charles Smith 

Women’s Committee: Seimone Augustus 

Men’s Veteran Committee: Dick Barnett

Women’s Veteran Committee: Harley Redin

International Committee: Michele Timms

Contributors Committee: Doug Collins, Herb Simon, Jerry West

“Without a shadow of doubt, the accomplishments of the 2024 class stand as a testament to unparalleled excellence,” said John L. Doleva, President and CEO of the Naismith Basketball Hall of Fame. “From showcasing unrivaled prowess on the courts and sidelines, spanning the high school, collegiate and professional ranks, to steering the course of basketball across an entire continent and to orchestrating a pair of dynasties, we are honored to pay tribute to these remarkable individuals alongside our esteemed award recipients.”

The Class of 2024 will be enshrined during festivities in Springfield, Mass., the Birthplace of Basketball, as well as the Mohegan Sun in Uncasville, Conn., on October 12-13. VIP packages and single-event tickets to the 2024 Hall of Fame Enshrinement Weekend are now available at hoophall.com/events/enshrinement-2024.

North American Committee Inductees

CHAUNCEY BILLUPS [Player] – Nicknamed “Mr. Big Shot” for his ability to deliver crucial plays in pivotal moments, Billups was a five-time NBA All-Star and the 2004 Finals MVP after leading the Detroit Pistons to their third NBA championship. During his 17-year career, Billups’ averaged 15.2 points and 5.4 assists per game, while his  89.4  free throw percentage is sixth best in NBA/ABA history. Billups was drafted third overall in 1997 by the Boston Celtics after earning Second Team All-American honors at Colorado. He is the current head coach of the Portland Trail Blazers.

VINCE CARTER [Player] – Renowned for his high-flying dunks, the former North Carolina standout was an eight-time All-Star in his 22 NBA seasons (1998-2020). Carter, who won the 2000 Slam Dunk Contest, was named the 1998-99 Rookie of the Year and is the only player in NBA history to play in four different decades. Carter averaged 16.7 points per game (21.3 as a starter) and also played a pivotal role in the success of the 2000 Sydney Olympics, clinching a gold medal with the U.S. Men’s Basketball Team. He currently holds the single-season franchise scoring record for the New Jersey/Brooklyn Nets and Toronto Raptors.

MICHAEL COOPER [Player] – Cooper was named to eight NBA All-Defensive Teams, taking home First-Team accolades five times and winning Defensive Player of the Year honors in 1987. The former third-round draft pick (60th overall) out of the University of New Mexico spent his entire 12-year NBA career (1978-90) with the Los Angeles Lakers, winning five championships while routinely matching up with the opponent’s top shooter. Following his playing career, Cooper entered the coaching ranks and earned WNBA Coach of the Year accolades in 2000 with the Los Angeles Sparks before winning back-to-back WNBA titles in 2001-02.

WALTER DAVIS [Player] – The all-time leading scorer in Phoenix Suns’ history was a six-time All-Star (1978-81, ‘84, ‘87) during his 15-year NBA career with the Suns (1977-88), Denver Nuggets (1988-92) and Portland Trail Blazers (1991). Before Phoenix chose Davis with the fifth pick in the 1977 draft, the standout player from North Carolina won a gold medal in the 1976 Summer Olympics. He then averaged 24.2 points per game in his first NBA season and was named the 1978 Rookie of the Year. The late Davis had his No. 6 retired by the Suns, and in 2004, he was enshrined in the team’s Ring of Honor.

BO RYAN [Coach] – Ryan went 747-233 (.762) in 32 seasons as a collegiate head coach with the University of Wisconsin-Platteville (1984-89), University of Wisconsin-Milwaukee (1999-2001) and University of Wisconsin (2001-15), including a 364-130 (.737) ledger in 14-plus seasons in Madison. Ryan, honored four times as the Big Ten Coach of the Year, led Wisconsin to unprecedented success, clinching four Big Ten regular-season titles, winning three Big Ten tournament championships, and making back-to-back Final Four appearances (2014-15). The four-time Division III champion (1991, ‘95, ‘98-99) was inducted into the College Basketball Hall of Fame in 2017.

CHARLES SMITH [Coach] – Smith is Louisiana’s all-time winningest high school head coach, surpassing the previous mark of 1,071 in 2020. With nine state championships to his name, Smith’s coaching tenure at Peabody Magnet High School began in 1985 after starting as a math teacher in 1975. He guided the Warhorses to a pair of perfect seasons, going 41-0 in 2004 and 2010 and earning national top-five rankings. Smith, ESPN’s National Coach of the Year in 2010, was inducted into the Louisiana Sports Hall of Fame in 2019 and the National High School Athletic Coaches Association Hall of Fame in 2021. In addition, Smith served as head coach at the McDonald’s All-American game in 2020.

Women’s Committee Inductee

SEIMONE AUGUSTUS [Player] – Augustus, who was selected first overall in the 2006 WNBA Draft, was a four-time champion and eight-time All-Star during her 15-year WNBA career (2006-20). The cornerstone player for the Minnesota Lynx, where she spent 14 of her 15 seasons, averaged 15.4 points per game and was named the 2006 Rookie of the Year and 2011 Finals MVP. At LSU, Augustus led the Tigers to three straight Final Four appearances and won the Naismith College Player of the Year and Wooden Awards in back-to-back campaigns (2005-06).

Men’s Veteran Committee Inductee

DICK BARNETT [Player] – Barnett first gained prominence at Tennessee A&I University, where he led the Tigers to three straight NAIA national championships (1957-59), the first historically black school to claim a men’s basketball title. The “Skull” played 14 NBA seasons with the Syracuse Nationals (1959-61), Los Angeles Lakers (1962-65) and New York Knicks (1965-73), winning a pair of titles in the Big Apple (1970, ‘73) and making his lone All-Star team in 1968. The Knicks retired his No. 12, and the Naismith Basketball Hall of Fame honored his 1957–59 Tigers squad in 2019.

Women’s Veteran Committee Inductee

HARLEY REDIN [Coach] – Redin went 431-66 in 18 seasons leading Wayland Baptist’s women’s basketball program and captured six AAU National Championships. He coached the Flying Queens to two undefeated women’s seasons (1956, ‘57), 17 top-five finishes, and went 110-2 during his first four campaigns at Wayland Baptist. The late Redin also coached the Women’s U.S. National Team in 1959, the 1971 Pan-American Games, and the 1963 World Tournament in Peru. He was the recipient of the Jostens-Berenson Service Award by the Women’s Basketball Coaches Association for his lifetime of service to women’s basketball in 1992, inducted into the Women’s Basketball Hall of Fame in 1999, and honored with the Naismith Award for Outstanding Contribution to the game of women’s basketball in 2000.

International Committee Inductee

MICHELE TIMMS [Player] – A trailblazer in Australian women’s basketball, Timms became well-known as one of the best point guards in the world, representing the Australian national team throughout the 1980s and 1990s. Timms won a pair of Olympic medals with the Opals, winning bronze in 1996 and silver in 2000, and was the first Australian, male or female, to play professional basketball internationally when she suited up for Lotus München in Germany. In addition, Timms spent five seasons with the Phoenix Mercury, guiding the team to a berth in the 1998 WNBA Finals and earning an All-Star selection in 1999. The Sport Australia Hall of Fame inducted her in 2003, the Women’s Basketball Hall of Fame in 2008, and the FIBA Hall of Fame in 2016. The Mercury retired her No. 7 jersey in 2002.

Contributor Committee Inductees

DOUG COLLINS – Collins’ versatile career in basketball is marked by significant achievements in roles ranging from player to coach to broadcaster, underscoring his wide-ranging impact on the sport. Following an NBA career where he was a four-time All-Star with the Philadelphia 76ers, he transitioned into coaching and accumulated over 400 wins with the Chicago Bulls (1986-89), Detroit Pistons (1995-98), Washington Wizards (2001-03) and 76ers (2010-13). Collins, who coached Hall of Famer Michael Jordan in both Chicago and Washington, also served as an analyst for various NBA-related broadcasts for CBS, NBC, TNT, TBS, and ABC/ESPN, along with working for NBC at the 2008 and 2012 Summer Olympics.

HERB SIMON – Simon, the longest-tenured governor in the history of the NBA, purchased the Indiana Pacers, along with his late brother Mel, back in 1983. Under Simon’s stewardship, the Pacers won numerous Central Division titles and made the franchise’s first NBA Finals in 2000. Throughout his ownership period, the Pacers have been home to legendary players like Jermaine O’Neal, Rik Smits, and Reggie Miller (Class of 2012). Beyond ownership, Simon’s influence extends deeply into the heart of the Pacers’ organization, shaping its identity and impact both on and off the court through his philanthropic endeavors and civic engagement.

JERRY WEST – Previously enshrined as a player and as a member of the 1960 U.S. Olympic Team, West then went on to establish himself off the court as one of the most successful executives in the sport’s history. West played a pivotal role as the architect of a pair of Los Angeles Lakers’ dynasties during the 1980s and 2000s, drafting key players like Magic Johnson and James Worthy, acquiring Kobe Bryant, signing free agent Shaquille O’Neal, and hiring head coach Phil Jackson. He totaled eight NBA championships in Los Angeles (1980, ‘82, ‘85, ‘87-88, 2000-02) and was named Executive of the Year twice (1995, 2004). Following his time in the Lakers’ front office (1979-2000), West served as GM of the Memphis Grizzlies from 2002-07 before earning two additional NBA championships as an executive with the Golden State Warriors (2015, ‘17). West is the first member of the Naismith Basketball Hall of Fame to be inducted as both a player and a contributor.

      <esi:otherwise>
        <esi:assign name="bpc">NONE</esi:assign>
      </esi:otherwise>
    </esi:choose>

        <esi:assign name="country">$url_encode($(GEO{'country_code'}))</esi:assign>

      <esi:when test="$(bpc)=='NONE'">
        <esi:assign name="country">$url_encode($(GEO{'country_code'}))</esi:assign>
      </esi:when>
      <esi:otherwise>
        <esi:assign name="country">$(bpc)</esi:assign>
      </esi:otherwise>
    </esi:choose>

    <esi:choose>
      <esi:when test="$(country)=='US'">
        <esi:assign name="isDomestic">true</esi:assign>
      </esi:when>
      <esi:otherwise>
        <esi:assign name="isDomestic">false</esi:assign>
      </esi:otherwise>
    </esi:choose>

The first round of the NBA Playoffs starts on April 18.</p></div><div class="ArticleHeader_ahBy__mKzod"><div class="ArticleHeader_ahAuthor__IteTb"><div class="ArticleAuthor_base__KkZ3k"><div class="ArticleAuthor_bio__ClsuO"><div><p class="ArticleAuthor_authorName__oeNmP">NBA.com Staff</p></div><div class="ArticleAuthor_authorSocial__pENOl"></div></div></div><time class="ArticleHeader_ahDate__sR9IG">Updated on April 16, 2026 7:16 AM</time></div></div></div><div class="Columns_col__b1RWg"><div class="Columns_left__7mcek"><div class="ArticleContent_article__bAlgR"><p><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" class="alignnone wp-image-2159106 size-large" src="https://cdn.nba.com/manage/2026/04/2026-playoffs-10-teams-logos-1568x882.png" alt="" width="684" height="384" srcset="https://cdn.nba.com/manage/2026/04/2026-playoffs-10-teams-logos-1568x882.png 1568w, https://cdn.nba.com/manage/2026/04/2026-playoffs-10-teams-logos-784x441.png 784w, https://cdn.nba.com/manage/2026/04/2026-playoffs-10-teams-logos-768x432.png 768w, https://cdn.nba.com/manage/2026/04/2026-playoffs-10-teams-logos-1536x864.png 1536w, https://cdn.nba.com/manage/2026/04/2026-playoffs-10-teams-logos-2048x1152.png 2048w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1568px) 100vw, 1568px"></p>
<p><strong>• </strong><a href="https://www.nba.com/news/nba-play-in-tournament"><strong>SoFi NBA Play-In Tournament</strong></a> | <a href="https://www.nba.com/playoffs/2026"><strong>Playoff Bracket</strong></a></p>
<p><em>This page will be updated each time postseason scheduling is officially announced. </em></p>
<hr>
<h2>2026 NBA Playoffs Dates To Know:</h2>
<ul>
<li>The 2026 SoFi NBA Play-In Tournament will take place <span style="color: #993300;"><strong>April 14-17</strong></span>.<strong><br>
</strong></li>
<li>The first round of the 2026 NBA Playoffs presented by Google will start <span style="color: #993300;"><strong>April 18</strong></span>.</li>
<li>Game 1 of the 2026 NBA Finals will be<span style="color: #993300;"><strong> June 3</strong></span>.</li>
</ul>
<hr>
<h2>> SoFi NBA Play-In Tournament</h2>
<p>The <a href="https://www.nba.com/playoffs/2026/play-in-tournament">2026 SoFi NBA Play-In Tournament</a> will take place April 14-17.<br>
All games will be broadcast exclusively on Prime Video.</p>
<p><span style="text-decoration: underline;"><strong>Tuesday, April 14</strong></span></p>
<ul>
<li><strong>East:</strong> <a href="https://www.nba.com/game/mia-vs-cha-0052500111">Hornets 127, Heat 126 (OT)</a> <em>(Loser eliminated)</em></li>
<li><strong>West</strong>: <a href="https://www.nba.com/game/por-vs-phx-0052500121">Trail Blazers 114, Suns 110</a><em> (Winner earns 7 seed)</em></li>
</ul>
<p><span style="text-decoration: underline;"><strong>Wednesday, April 15</strong></span></p>
<ul>
<li><strong>East:</strong> <a href="https://www.nba.com/game/orl-vs-phi-0052500101">76ers 109, Magic 97</a><em> (Winner earns 7 seed)</em></li>
<li><strong>West:</strong> <a href="https://www.nba.com/game/gsw-vs-lac-0052500131">Warriors 126, Clippers 121</a><em> (Loser eliminated)</em></li>
</ul>
<p><span style="text-decoration: underline;"><strong>Friday, April 17</strong></span></p>
<ul>
<li><strong>East:</strong> <a href="https://www.nba.com/game/0052500201">Magic vs. Hornets</a>, <em>7:30 p.m. ET (Winner earns 8 seed)</em></li>
<li><strong>West:</strong> <a href="https://www.nba.com/game/0052500211">Suns vs. Warriors</a>, <em>10 p.m. ET (Winner earns 8 seed)</em></li>
</ul>
<p> </p>
<hr>
<h2>> First Round</h2>
<p>The schedule for the first round of the NBA Playoffs will be announced at the conclusion of the regular season.</p>

2026 NBA Play-in, Playoffs & Finals Schedule
The 2026 SoFi NBA Play-In Tournament will take place on April 14-17. The first round of the NBA Playoffs starts on April 18.

• SoFi NBA Play-In Tournament | Playoff Bracket

This page will be updated each time postseason scheduling is officially announced. 

2026 NBA Playoffs Dates To Know:
The 2026 SoFi NBA Play-In Tournament will take place April 14-17.
The first round of the 2026 NBA Playoffs presented by Google will start April 18.
Game 1 of the 2026 NBA Finals will be June 3.
> SoFi NBA Play-In Tournament
The 2026 SoFi NBA Play-In Tournament will take place April 14-17.
All games will be broadcast exclusively on Prime Video.

Tuesday, April 14

East: Hornets 127, Heat 126 (OT) (Loser eliminated)
West: Trail Blazers 114, Suns 110 (Winner earns 7 seed)
Wednesday, April 15

East: 76ers 109, Magic 97 (Winner earns 7 seed)
West: Warriors 126, Clippers 121 (Loser eliminated)
Friday, April 17

East: Magic vs. Hornets, 7:30 p.m. ET (Winner earns 8 seed)
West: Suns vs. Warriors, 10 p.m. ET (Winner earns 8 seed)
 

> First Round
The schedule for the first round of the NBA Playoffs will be announced at the conclusion of the regular season.

Eastern Conference

(1) Detroit vs. (8) TBD
Game 1: TBD at Detroit | Sunday April 19 (6:30 ET, NBC/Peacock)
Game 2: TBD at Detroit | Wednesday April 22 (7 ET, ESPN)
Game 3: Detroit at TBD | Saturday April 25 (1 ET, NBC/Peacock)
Game 4: Detroit at TBD | Monday April 27
Game 5: TBD at Detroit* | Wednesday April 29
Game 6: Detroit at TBD* | Friday May 1
Game 7: TBD at Detroit* | Sunday May 3
(2) Boston vs. (7) Philadelphia
Game 1: Philadelphia at Boston | Sunday April 19 (1 ET, ABC)
Game 2: Philadelphia at Boston | Tuesday April 21 (7 ET, NBC/Peacock)
Game 3: Boston at Philadelphia | Friday April 24 (7 ET, Prime Video)
Game 4: Boston at Philadelphia | Sunday April 26 (7 ET, NBC)
Game 5: Philadelphia at Boston* | Tuesday April 28
Game 6: Boston at Philadelphia* | Thursday April 30
Game 7: Philadelphia at Boston* | Saturday May 2
(3) New York vs. (6) Atlanta
Game 1: Atlanta at New York | Saturday April 18 (6 ET, Prime Video)
Game 2: Atlanta at New York | Monday April 20 (8 ET, NBC)
Game 3: New York at Atlanta | Thursday April 23 (7 ET, Prime Video)
Game 4: New York at Atlanta | Saturday April 25 (6 ET, NBC)
Game 5: Atlanta at New York | Tuesday April 28
Game 6: New York at Atlanta | Thursday April 30
Game 7: Atlanta at New York | Saturday May 2
(4) Cleveland vs. (5) Toronto
Game 1: Toronto at Cleveland | Saturday April 18 (1 ET, Prime Video)
Game 2: Toronto at Cleveland | Monday April 20 (7 ET, NBC/Peacock)
Game 3: Cleveland at Toronto | Thursday April 23 (8 ET, Prime Video)
Game 4: Cleveland at Toronto | Sunday April 26 (1 ET, ESPN)
Game 5: Toronto at Cleveland | Wednesday April 29
Game 6: Cleveland at Toronto | Friday May 1
Game 7: Toronto at Cleveland | Sunday May 3
Western Conference

(1) Oklahoma City vs. (8) TBD
Game 1: TBD at Oklahoma City | Sunday April 19 (3:30 ET, ABC)
Game 2: TBD at Oklahoma City | Wednesday April 22 (9:30 ET, ESPN)
Game 3: Oklahoma City at TBD | Saturday April 25 (3:30 ET, NBC)
Game 4: Oklahoma City at TBD | Monday April 27
Game 5: TBD at Oklahoma City | Wednesday April 29
Game 6: Oklahoma City at TBD | Friday May 1
Game 7: TBD at Oklahoma City | Sunday May 3
(2) San Antonio vs. (7) Portland
Game 1: Portland at San Antonio | Sunday April 19 (9 ET, NBC/Peacock)
Game 2: Portland at San Antonio | Tuesday April 21 (8 ET, NBC)
Game 3: San Antonio at Portland | Friday April 24 (10:30 ET, Prime Video)
Game 4: San Antonio at Portland | Sunday April 26 (3:30 ET, ESPN)
Game 5: Portland at San Antonio | Tuesday April 28
Game 6: San Antonio at Portland | Thursday April 30
Game 7: Portland at San Antonio | Saturday May 2
(3) Denver vs. (6) Minnesota
Game 1: Minnesota at Denver | Saturday April 18 (3:30 ET, Prime Video)
Game 2: Minnesota at Denver | Monday April 20 (10:30 ET, NBC)
Game 3: Denver at Minnesota | Thursday April 23 (9:30 ET, Prime Video)
Game 4: Denver at Minnesota | Saturday April 25 (8:30 ET, ABC)
Game 5: Minnesota at Denver | Monday April 27
Game 6: Denver at Minnesota | Thursday April 30
Game 7: Minnesota at Denver | Saturday May 2
(4) Los Angeles vs. (5) Houston
Game 1: Houston at Los Angeles | Saturday April 18 (8:30 ET, ABC)
Game 2: Houston at Los Angeles | Tuesday April 21 (10:30 ET, NBC)
Game 3: Los Angeles at Houston | Friday April 24 (8 ET, Prime Video)
Game 4: Los Angeles at Houston | Sunday April 26 (9:30 ET, NBC)
Game 5: Houston at Los Angeles | Wednesday April 29
Game 6: Los Angeles at Houston | Friday May 1
Game 7: Houston at Los Angeles | Sunday May 3
* = If necessary

> Conference Semifinals
The Conference Semifinals schedule will be updated at the conclusion of the previous round.

 

> Conference Finals
The Conference Finals schedule will be updated at the conclusion of the previous round.

 

> 2026 NBA Finals
The 2026 NBA Finals will be begin on June 3, with ABC as the exclusive broadcaster.

June 3: NBA Finals 2026 – Game 1 on ABC, 8:30 p.m. ET
June 5: NBA Finals 2026 – Game 2 on ABC, 8:30 p.m. ET
June 8: NBA Finals 2026 – Game 3 on ABC, 8:30 p.m. ET
June 10: NBA Finals 2026 – Game 4 on ABC, 8:30 p.m. ET
June 13: NBA Finals 2026 – Game 5 on ABC, 8:30 p.m. ET (if necessary)
June 16: NBA Finals 2026 – Game 6 on ABC, 8:30 p.m. ET (if necessary)
June 19: NBA Finals 2026 – Game 7 on ABC, 8:30 p.m. ET (if necessary)

          </script><script id="tealium-script" type="text/javascript" async="" src="https://tags.nba.com/nba-web/prod/utag.js"></script><script type="application/ld+json">{"@context":"https://schema.org/","@type":"Article","@id":"https://www.nba.com/news/kia-rookie-ladder-jan-14-2026","headline":"Kia Rookie Ladder: Durable rookie class keeps impressing","url":"https://www.nba.com/news/kia-rookie-ladder-jan-14-2026","articleBody":"Not only have the Dallas Mavericks’ Cooper Flagg and the Charlotte Hornets' Kon Knueppel excelled on the court to earn top spots here at the Kia Rookie Ladder, but they have mostly stayed on the court, too.  \r\n\r\nEach has missed just one game so far, which is owed to their youth and spry physical shape. But it also speaks to their mental approach to surviving minor dings and pushing through without any string of absences.  \r\n\r\nRecently, the two former Duke teammates spoke separately about the grind and how they try to push through.  \r\n\r\n“I feel solid,” Flagg said when asked about his Mavericks workload. “It’s a lot of basketball, but I’m figuring out my routine — what works and what doesn’t. Some days you feel tired, some days you don’t feel 100 percent, but you play through it and figure it out.” \r\n\r\nSaid Knueppel: “I think you just have to be willing to play. ... Going in with that mindset: How can I have my body ready to play and perform every day? \r\n\r\n“Like, not ‘Oh no, my ankle hurts, I've got to get some treatment today.’ It's more like, ‘Alright, my shoulder tends to bug me, let's get working on the shoulder throughout the week.’”  \r\n\r\nSome form of injury accommodation is likely in both players’ future, but they’re not load-managing now, which gives maximum exposure to ticket-buying and TV-viewing fans around the league.  \r\n\r\nHere is this week’s Kia Rookie Ladder of the league’s top newcomers:  \r\nWeekly recap\r\n• Typically, this is a column where veteran players and coaches offer compliments and impressions of the new guys. But Philadelphia 76ers rookie VJ Edgecombe turned the tables recently when talking about teammate Kyle Lowry, whom he considers essential as a mentor. The young guard was delighted to see how much appreciation Lowry got when Philadelphia played at Toronto this week. “That was amazing,” he said. “The city shows that they still love him. … I was at home when they won the [2019] championship, so, like I said, for them to show love, credit to them. Credit to them for not being salty or anything.” \r\n\r\n• Special mention to Toronto’s Collin Murray-Boyles, who has been rolling in his past six games: 10.5 ppg, 8.2 rpg, 3.7 apg, two steals and one block, while shooting 60.9% and helping the Raptors go 4-2.  \r\n\r\n• Ryan Nembhard’s work off the bench for Dallas hadn’t been great (3.9 ppg, 21.4% 3-point shooting), but that’s where he’ll likely get his minutes once Kyrie Irving is back. So his play in reserve Monday in Brooklyn (nine points, eight assists, one turnover in 22 minutes) was a good sign.  \r\n\r\n\r\n\r\nStoryline to watch  \r\nBeware the rookie wall. This is the time of year rookies make like bugs drawn to one of those patio purple lights: Zap! The games have come fast and hard, rest has been sketchy and young fellas more used to playing 32 games in a whole season in college are pushing toward 40 or more in less than three months. One early player who seems to have gotten a rude introduction to the wall is San Antonio’s Dylan Harper, who went into Tuesday’s game at OKC averaging 2.3 points, three rebounds and 1.3 assists for the week, while shooting 16.7%. How Harper bounces back, and how many others in the Class of 2025 share a similar experience, will be something to watch.  \r\n\r\n\r\n\r\n(All stats through Tuesday, Jan. 13) \r\n1. Cooper Flagg, Dallas Mavericks\r\nSeason stats: 19.1 ppg, 6.4 rpg, 4.3 apg\r\nLast Ladder: No. 1 ↔️\r\nDraft pick: No. 1 \r\n\r\nIt’s hard to find a flaw in Flagg’s game -- frankly, his overall approach to this season as a teenage sensation -- that he doesn’t address and fix. How ‘bout his work against Brooklyn Monday, when he notched his sixth game with at least 25 points, five rebounds and five assists – and this time didn’t turn over the ball in 35 minutes, while chipping in three steals and a block?  \r\n\r\n\r\n\r\n2. Kon Knueppel, Charlotte Hornets\r\nSeason stats: 19.1 ppg, 5.1 rpg, 3.6 apg \r\nLast Ladder: No. 2 ↔️\r\nDraft pick: No. 4 \r\n\r\nKnueppel looked a little vulnerable for slipping off this No. 2 rung on which he’s signed his name for much of the season, given his 30.4% shooting on 3-pointers and the end of his streak of games with multiple 3-pointers. But he did enough to hang on, and does enough regularly to impress another opponent. Here’s Clippers coach Ty Lue on Monday talking about the Charlotte forward: “He plays at his own pace. He’s a 3-level scorer, he can get to the basket, mid-range, shoots the 3, and understanding how to play the game. He doesn’t force anything. … He was just too comfortable last game we played him.” \r\n\r\n\r\n\r\n3. VJ Edgecombe, Philadelphia 76ers\r\nSeason stats: 16.1 ppg, 5.4 rpg, 4.4 apg \r\nLast Ladder: No. 3 ↔️\r\nDraft pick: No. 3 \r\n\r\nEdgecombe couldn’t quite overtake Knueppel this week, but the Sixers weren’t quibbling with his production, which included an average of 6.5 assists this week. He logged 38.2 minutes nightly and helped Philadelphia win three of four.  \r\n\r\n\r\n\r\n4. Derik Queen, New Orleans Pelicans\r\nSeason stats: 12.8 ppg, 7.5 rpg, 4.3 apg\r\nLast Ladder: No. 4 ↔️\r\nDraft pick: No. 13 \r\n\r\nPraise from a kindred big-man spirit, Pelicans assistant coach Greg Monroe, reiterated what a lot of rookie observers have noticed: this kid is completely unruffled against the world’s best competition. Said Monroe: “He’s not intimidated. … There’s a moment when you think, ‘Wow, I’m actually playing against this dude.’ But he is not intimidated by anyone, and he is not lacking in confidence.” Queen, by the way, got his second triple-double of 14, 16 and 12 on his return to his roots at Washington on Friday -- and he’s the only rookie who’s had even one.  \r\n\r\n\r\n\r\n5. Cedric Coward, Memphis Grizzlies\r\nSeason stats: 13.9 ppg, 6.7 rpg, 2.9 apg \r\nLast Ladder: No. 5 ↔️\r\nDraft pick: No. 11 \r\n\r\nThanks in part to his 46.2% shooting on 3-pointers, Coward averaged 17 points, 8.5 boards and 2.5 assists this week, while taking 6.5 of his 12 field-goal attempts from deep. He ranks second to Queen among the rookies in rebounding, and he scored in double figures 13 times in his past 14 games.  \r\n\r\n\r\n\r\n The next 5\r\n6. Maxime Raynaud, Sacramento Kings \r\n\r\nSeason stats: 10.5 ppg, 6.5 rpg, 1.1 apg \r\nLast Ladder: No. 7 ⬆️\r\nDraft pick: No. 42 \r\n\r\nWhen you’re a big man with skills and you’ve had the impact Raynaud has had (despite being drafted in the middle of the second round), teams -- or at least some of their fans -- might be second-guessing themselves. Minnesota is one such team, as it drafted a pair of project centers in June (Joan Beringer and Rocco Zikarsky), but could have had Raynaud for more current help.  \r\n\r\n7. Egor Dëmin, Brooklyn Nets  \r\n\r\nSeason stats: 10.4 ppg, 3.3 rpg, 3.5 apg\r\nLast Ladder: No. 9 ⬆️\r\nDraft pick: No. 8  \r\n\r\nIn the half dozen games right before Dëmin sat out vs. Dallas on Monday, he had drained 24 of 46 3-pointers, a 52.2% clip. Curiously, he shot 4-of-19 from inside the arc in that stretch of games.  \r\n\r\n8. Jeremiah Fears, New Orleans Pelicans \r\n\r\nSeason stats: 14.4 ppg, 3.6 rpg, 3.1 apg\r\nLast Ladder: No. 8 ↔️\r\nDraft pick: No. 7 \r\n\r\nFears is back to consistently scoring 10+ per game after a spate of single-digit performances around the holidays. He has averaged 16 points in his past six appearances -- the rookie point guard has yet to miss a game -- and had his share of moments like this.  \r\n\r\n9. Caleb Love, Portland Trail Blazers \r\n\r\nSeason stats: 10.7 ppg, 2.7 rpg, 2.3 apg\r\nLast Ladder: Not ranked ⬆️\r\nDraft pick: Undrafted \r\n\r\nIn early December, Love was hearing the word “inefficient” attached to his performances for Portland. But it’s hard to quibble with his production lately. Since Dec. 23, he has averaged 16.8 points on 45% shooting, including 40.4% on 3-pointers. The word he’s starting to hear now: “Lillard.”  \r\n\r\n10. Tre Johnson, Washington Wizards\r\n\r\nSeason stats: 12.5 ppg, 3 rpg, 1.9 apg \r\nLast Ladder: No. 10 ↔️\r\nDraft pick: No. 6  \r\n\r\nJohnson continues to up his scoring for Washington, and earned praise from the Phoenix Suns' Devin Booker after Johnson scored 19 in the Wizards’ road loss to the Suns on Sunday. “He's smooth. He can shoot the ball. He can do a little bit of everything,” Booker said. Of hearing the rookie compared to him, Booker added: “I'll take that after what he was doing tonight. He looks like a special talent.” \r\n\r\n* * *\r\n\r\nSteve Aschburner has written about the NBA since 1980. You can e-mail him here, find his archive here and follow him on X.","dateCreated":"2026-01-14T13:00:24Z","description":"Cooper Flagg and Kon Knueppel are among the rookies showing resilience and availability all season long.","thumbnailUrl":"https://cdn.nba.com/manage/2026/01/GettyImages-2255996992-scaled-e1768277305103.jpg","articleSection":"Kia Rookie
