Powell","firstName":"Shaun","lastName":"Powell","organization":"","isNBAStaff":false,"authorPhoto":"https://cdn.nba.com/manage/2020/10/Powell.jpg","socialHandle":"","socialLink":"","wpseoTitle":"","wpseoMetadesc":"","description":"","userUrl":"","featuredAuthor":true},"contentText":"There’s often a tendency to label the post-All-Star break as the dog days of the regular season schedule, although this can also be misleading to a point. What if these dog days have bark? \r\nThe season isn’t over, and in some respects, a segment of the season is just beginning. There’s still so much up for grabs, so much unsettled, so much at stake. And it’ll require a month or maybe more to resolve. \r\nThe stretch run is where the championship contenders separate themselves from the pack and where the gap begins to expand between playoff hopefuls. Or maybe those gaps don’t expand; maybe they need an 82nd game. \r\nThese are the 10 pressing issues and developments waiting to be unraveled between now and mid-April — if not beyond:\r\n\r\n1. Jayson Tatum's Decision\r\nThis is by far the most substantial stretch-run must-see because of its obvious, far-reaching implications. If Tatum announces a return to the Boston Celtics— and even more, displays little to no rust — it could disrupt the power structure of the East and the league as well. \r\nThat’s how much Tatum, star of the 2024 championship run and All-NBA player out since injuring his Achilles last spring, means to the Celtics and how much a difference-maker of his magnitude can make in the playoffs. With a boost from Jaylen Brown, the Celtics are flourishing anyway without Tatum, currently at 35-19 and in second place. \r\nTatum has begun light workouts in a clear indication of an attempted return. If that happens, it must take place no later than March; Tatum would require multiple tune-ups to prepare for the intensity of the playoffs. \r\nBrown said Tatum is “checking off all the right boxes,” and Kevin Durant, who successfully returned from the same injury five years ago, has high hopes for Tatum. \r\n“I expect to see All-Star level play and the same Jayson Tatum we've seen before he was injured,” Durant said. “Usually around this time, you're playing a lot of five-on-five, playing against live bodies, just trying to get your rhythm and tempo back. So I'm looking forward to seeing him get out there with his team.” \r\n\r\n2. OKC at Full Strength\r\nThe 2025-26 season has been a unique one, to say the least, for the defending champion Thunder. They ripped through a 24-1 start and created comparisons with the 73-win Warriors. On the flip side, they’ve been spooked by the San Antonio Spurs, who have beaten OKC four times. \r\nThrough it all, OKC has never been fully healthy, with Jalen Williams missing 30 games, Isaiah Hartenstein 16 straight from December through January and Shai Gilgeous-Alexander the last five. \r\nThat’s about to change, and just in time. Williams is starting to regain his timing following off-season wrist surgery; Hartenstein is back from a calf strain and Shai’s abdominal injury was never projected to keep him from the lineup for a substantial stretch. \r\nWith a complete rotation that features the reigning MVP, solid defense and stretchy depth that added Jared McCain at the trade deadline, can the Thunder finish the season the way they started it and ride that momentum into the playoffs? \r\n\r\n3. Denver Looking for Dominance\r\nAn off-season of change helped reshape the Nuggets and position them for another attempted takeover of the West, helped largely by three-time MVP Nikola Jokić. That process remains on hold, though, because the Nuggets are still trying to find their footing and traction. \r\nJokić missed nearly a month after suffering a hyperextended knee, and the Nuggets’ rhythm has been off since his return; Denver is just 3-5 (although one loss was in overtime and two others were by a combined five points). While they’re getting positive play from Peyton Watson in an upgraded role, Aaron Gordon won’t be re-evaluated for another few weeks for his hamstring injury. \r\nSo, the Nuggets aren’t whole just yet. However, there’s upside. They’re on the clock, though, because the West is loaded and the margin for error just shrank. \r\n\r\n4. The Cleveland Connection\r\nDonovan Mitchell’s new backcourt running partner is James Harden and the honeymoon is blissful. The Cavs are undefeated in three games with Harden and are displaying a different dynamic. \r\nHarden is ninth on the all-time career scoring list yet seems more willing to rely on his passing skills, and perhaps justifiably so. Mitchell is a high-volume shooter and the other Cavs — Evan Mobley and Jarrett Allen in particular — require a creator. Harden is very good in that role; he worked wonders with Ivica Zubac with the Clippers, turning Zubac into a primary scoring option. Harden is averaging over nine assists with the Cavs. \r\nThis represents a major philosophical change by a contender in the middle of the season. Usually, these hasty transitional situations either don’t work or require more time. In this case, Harden might just be what Mitchell and the Cavs ordered. \r\n\r\n5. The Hornets' Hum\r\nIf there were an award for emerging out of nowhere, then the Charlotte Hornets would be a runaway winner. This team was dormant and stuck in the mud until right after the holidays. Suddenly the switch flipped and the Hornets took themselves out of the draft lottery conversation, and here they are, in the thick of play-in territory and rising. \r\nThe surprise is that the Hornets have done this without a major acquisition at the trade deadline. Their development is strictly the result of in-house growth and obviously the immediate impact of Kon Knueppel, a favorite for Rookie of the Year and already one of the league’s best shooters. \r\nLaMelo Ball is healthy and frisky. Charlotte has four players who can get 20 or more points a night — Ball, Knueppel, Brandon Miller, and Miles Bridges. The Hornets have won 10 of their last 11 with signature wins over the Spurs and Rockets. A seven-game home stand next month will tell; the Knicks, Sixers and Celtics (perhaps with Tatum) will pay a visit to an arena that’s suddenly swelling with noise. \r\n\r\n6. The Bucks Try to Bounce Back\r\nIt wasn’t too long ago when the Bucks seemed on the verge of collapse. The season started poorly, Myles Turner’s impact was marginal at best, they lost nine of 10 and then Giannis Antetokounmpo gave off weird vibes leading up to the trade deadline and while tending to a calf injury. \r\nGiannis wasn’t swapped at the deadline and the best the Bucks could do to improve the roster was sift through the bargain bin and add some players on the cheap. \r\nWell, Cam Thomas and Ousmane Dieng seem like perfect fits for a team that needed scoring and an emerging young talent. Meanwhile, Ryan Rollins and Kevin Porter Jr. are still making the most of their upgraded roles in the rotation. \r\nGiannis is due back any day from his injury. He remains a premier talent, and if he blends with his new surroundings, the Bucks, winners of five of their last six games without Giannis, could be a team no one wants to see in the playoffs. Provided Milwaukee gets there, of course. \r\n\r\n7. MVP Race\r\nThere's an interesting game on April 10, a final week tip-off that otherwise would seem relatively meaningless for two teams headed for the playoffs. Except: It’s OKC vs. Denver, meaning Shai vs. Jokić. Might the MVP race still be a toss-up at such a late stage? \r\nShai and Jokić are front-runners at the moment. Others are in the mix as well, but this race appears to be a carbon copy of last season’s when Shai emerged as the winner, mainly because OKC ran away with the best record in the West. There’s no such cushion this time between the teams. Therefore, suspense awaits. \r\n\r\n8. Rookie Race\r\nHas Cooper Flagg wrapped up the Rookie of the Year award? It appears so. There’s an argument that Flagg should’ve been the first rookie since Blake Griffin to make the All-Star Game (he didn’t, despite averaging 20 points and six rebounds). That’s how impactful he is for the Dallas Mavericks, and why the Mavs have already taken steps (trading Anthony Davis) to begin the process of building around their new centerpiece. \r\nIt will be Flagg or his Duke teammate, Knueppel, who’s averaging 19 points and shooting 43% from deep, taking honors. Actually, the only question is this: Why didn’t Duke win the national title with those two? \r\n\r\n9. Key Games in the Final Two Weeks\r\nSeveral teams will get pressed in the stretch run because they’ll either be boxing out for playoff seeding, trying to avoid the play-in tournament or trying to make the play-in cut.\r\nBecause of that, there’s almost guaranteed to be drama right before the close of the regular season. Here are four games to circle: \r\n\r\n\r\n \tPistons at Thunder, Mar. 30: Will these teams still hold a lead in their respective conferences? Being a top seed has its rewards. \r\n \tSpurs and Cavs at Warriors, April 1 and 2: This back-to-back could serve as the last stand for Stephen Curry, this season anyway, as the Warriors try to make a playoff push. They’re currently in eighth place and seemingly locked in the play-in tournament at the moment. \r\n \tRockets at Suns, April 7: Durant will see his old mates in a game that might drip with implications. Both Houston and Phoenix, along with others, are flirting with the sixth-place cutoff for guaranteed playoff spots; the Suns and Rockets are currently separated by two games in the West. \r\n \tCeltics at Knicks, April 9: These rivals are currently ranked second and third in the East. Remember, the second seed has home court advantage until the conference finals, and maybe even then, should the top seed tumble. \r\n\r\n10. LeBron and the Lakers\r\nIs the end approaching for a legend and a legendary franchise? LeBron James remains mum about his future plans; he can become a free agent this summer. Also, with a change in ownership, there has been no hint from the organization regarding where it stands on the subject. \r\nSo everything’s cloudy in LA. Also uncertain is the Lakers’ stretch run and how far it stretches. LeBron and Luka Dončić are All-Stars and the chemistry between the two seems fine. Austin Reaves is healthy again and impactful. But partly because of injuries, the Lakers haven’t given off contending vibes because defense and depth remain problematic. \r\n“When we've played some of our best basketball of the season, we've looked very good,” he said. “On the other side, when we've been terrible, we've looked disgusting.” \r\nWhenever the season ends in LA, only one question will beg for an answer: What next, LeBron? \r\n“I want to live,” he said over the weekend. “When I know, you guys will know. I don't know. I have no idea. I just want to live. That's all.” \r\n* * *\r\n\r\nShaun Powell has covered the NBA since 1985. There’s still so much up for grabs, so much unsettled, so much at stake. And it’ll require a month or maybe more to resolve.\u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan data-ccp-props=\"{\u0026quot;134245417\u0026quot;:true,\u0026quot;134245418\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;134245529\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;201341983\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335551550\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335551620\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335557856\u0026quot;:4278190080,\u0026quot;335559685\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559731\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559737\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559738\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559739\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559740\u0026quot;:240}\"\u003e \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp data-ccp-border-bottom=\"0px none #000000\" data-ccp-padding-bottom=\"0px\" data-ccp-border-between=\"0px none #000000\" data-ccp-padding-between=\"0px\"\u003e\u003cspan data-contrast=\"none\"\u003eThe stretch run is where the championship contenders separate themselves from the pack and where the gap begins to expand between playoff hopefuls. Or maybe those gaps don’t expand; maybe they need an 82nd game.\u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan data-ccp-props=\"{\u0026quot;134245417\u0026quot;:true,\u0026quot;134245418\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;134245529\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;201341983\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335551550\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335551620\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335557856\u0026quot;:4278190080,\u0026quot;335559685\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559731\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559737\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559738\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559739\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559740\u0026quot;:240}\"\u003e \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp data-ccp-border-bottom=\"0px none #000000\" data-ccp-padding-bottom=\"0px\" data-ccp-border-between=\"0px none #000000\" data-ccp-padding-between=\"0px\"\u003e\u003cspan data-contrast=\"none\"\u003eThese are the 10 pressing issues and developments waiting to be unraveled between now and mid-April — if not beyond:\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003ch3\u003e1. Jayson Tatum\u0026#8217;s Decision\u003c/h3\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan data-contrast=\"none\"\u003eThis is by far the most substantial stretch-run must-see because of its obvious, far-reaching implications. If Tatum announces a return to the Boston Celtics— and even more, displays little to no rust — it could disrupt the power structure of the East and the league as well.\u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan data-ccp-props=\"{\u0026quot;134245417\u0026quot;:true,\u0026quot;134245418\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;134245529\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;201341983\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335551550\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335551620\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335557856\u0026quot;:4278190080,\u0026quot;335559685\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559731\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559737\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559738\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559739\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559740\u0026quot;:240}\"\u003e \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp data-ccp-border-bottom=\"0px none #000000\" data-ccp-padding-bottom=\"0px\" data-ccp-border-between=\"0px none #000000\" data-ccp-padding-between=\"0px\"\u003e\u003cspan data-contrast=\"none\"\u003eThat’s how much Tatum, star of the 2024 championship run and All-NBA player out since injuring his Achilles last spring, means to the Celtics and how much a difference-maker of his magnitude can make in the playoffs. With a boost from Jaylen Brown, the Celtics are flourishing anyway without Tatum, currently at 35-19 and in second place.\u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan data-ccp-props=\"{\u0026quot;134245417\u0026quot;:true,\u0026quot;134245418\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;134245529\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;201341983\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335551550\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335551620\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335557856\u0026quot;:4278190080,\u0026quot;335559685\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559731\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559737\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559738\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559739\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559740\u0026quot;:240}\"\u003e \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp data-ccp-border-bottom=\"0px none #000000\" data-ccp-padding-bottom=\"0px\" data-ccp-border-between=\"0px none #000000\" data-ccp-padding-between=\"0px\"\u003e\u003cspan data-contrast=\"none\"\u003eTatum has begun light workouts in a clear indication of an attempted return. Williams is starting to regain his timing following off-season wrist surgery; Hartenstein is back from a calf strain and Shai’s abdominal injury was never projected to keep him from the lineup for a substantial stretch.\u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan data-ccp-props=\"{\u0026quot;134245417\u0026quot;:true,\u0026quot;134245418\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;134245529\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;201341983\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335551550\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335551620\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335557856\u0026quot;:4278190080,\u0026quot;335559685\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559731\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559737\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559738\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559739\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559740\u0026quot;:240}\"\u003e \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp data-ccp-border-bottom=\"0px none #000000\" data-ccp-padding-bottom=\"0px\" data-ccp-border-between=\"0px none #000000\" data-ccp-padding-between=\"0px\"\u003e\u003cspan data-contrast=\"none\"\u003eWith a complete rotation that features the reigning MVP, solid defense and stretchy depth that added Jared McCain at the trade deadline, can the Thunder finish the season the way they started it and ride that momentum into the playoffs?\u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan data-ccp-props=\"{\u0026quot;134245417\u0026quot;:true,\u0026quot;134245418\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;134245529\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;201341983\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335551550\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335551620\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335557856\u0026quot;:4278190080,\u0026quot;335559685\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559731\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559737\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559738\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559739\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559740\u0026quot;:240}\"\u003e \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003ch3\u003e3. Denver Looking for Dominance\u003c/h3\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan data-contrast=\"none\"\u003eAn off-season of change helped reshape the Nuggets and position them for another attempted takeover of the West, helped largely by three-time MVP Nikola Jokić. That process remains on hold, though, because the Nuggets are still trying to find their footing and traction.\u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan data-ccp-props=\"{\u0026quot;134245417\u0026quot;:true,\u0026quot;134245418\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;134245529\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;201341983\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335551550\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335551620\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335557856\u0026quot;:4278190080,\u0026quot;335559685\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559731\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559737\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559738\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559739\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559740\u0026quot;:240}\"\u003e \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp data-ccp-border-bottom=\"0px none #000000\" data-ccp-padding-bottom=\"0px\" data-ccp-border-between=\"0px none #000000\" data-ccp-padding-between=\"0px\"\u003e\u003cspan data-contrast=\"none\"\u003eJokić missed nearly a month after suffering a hyperextended knee, and the Nuggets’ rhythm has been off since his return; Denver is just 3-5 (although one loss was in overtime and two others were by a combined five points). While they’re getting positive play from Peyton Watson in an upgraded role, Aaron Gordon won’t be re-evaluated for another few weeks for his hamstring injury.\u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan data-ccp-props=\"{\u0026quot;134245417\u0026quot;:true,\u0026quot;134245418\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;134245529\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;201341983\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335551550\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335551620\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335557856\u0026quot;:4278190080,\u0026quot;335559685\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559731\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559737\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559738\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559739\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559740\u0026quot;:240}\"\u003e \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp data-ccp-border-bottom=\"0px none #000000\" data-ccp-padding-bottom=\"0px\" data-ccp-border-between=\"0px none #000000\" data-ccp-padding-between=\"0px\"\u003e\u003cspan data-contrast=\"none\"\u003eSo, the Nuggets aren’t whole just yet. However, there’s upside. They’re on the clock, though, because the West is loaded and the margin for error just shrank.\u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan data-ccp-props=\"{\u0026quot;134245417\u0026quot;:true,\u0026quot;134245418\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;134245529\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;201341983\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335551550\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335551620\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335557856\u0026quot;:4278190080,\u0026quot;335559685\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559731\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559737\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559738\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559739\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559740\u0026quot;:240}\"\u003e \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003ch3\u003e4. The Cleveland Connection\u003c/h3\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan data-contrast=\"none\"\u003eDonovan Mitchell’s new backcourt running partner is James Harden and the honeymoon is blissful. The Cavs are undefeated in three games with Harden and are displaying a different dynamic.\u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan data-ccp-props=\"{\u0026quot;134245417\u0026quot;:true,\u0026quot;134245418\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;134245529\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;201341983\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335551550\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335551620\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335557856\u0026quot;:4278190080,\u0026quot;335559685\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559731\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559737\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559738\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559739\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559740\u0026quot;:240}\"\u003e \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp data-ccp-border-bottom=\"0px none #000000\" data-ccp-padding-bottom=\"0px\" data-ccp-border-between=\"0px none #000000\" data-ccp-padding-between=\"0px\"\u003e\u003cspan data-contrast=\"none\"\u003eHarden is ninth on the all-time career scoring list yet seems more willing to rely on his passing skills, and perhaps justifiably so. Mitchell is a high-volume shooter and the other Cavs — Evan Mobley and Jarrett Allen in particular — require a creator. Harden is very good in that role; he worked wonders with Ivica Zubac with the Clippers, turning Zubac into a primary scoring option. Harden is averaging over nine assists with the Cavs.\u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan data-ccp-props=\"{\u0026quot;134245417\u0026quot;:true,\u0026quot;134245418\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;134245529\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;201341983\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335551550\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335551620\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335557856\u0026quot;:4278190080,\u0026quot;335559685\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559731\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559737\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559738\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559739\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559740\u0026quot;:240}\"\u003e \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp data-ccp-border-bottom=\"0px none #000000\" data-ccp-padding-bottom=\"0px\" data-ccp-border-between=\"0px none #000000\" data-ccp-padding-between=\"0px\"\u003e\u003cspan data-contrast=\"none\"\u003eThis represents a major philosophical change by a contender in the middle of the season. Usually, these hasty transitional situations either don’t work or require more time. In this case, Harden might just be what Mitchell and the Cavs ordered.\u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan data-ccp-props=\"{\u0026quot;134245417\u0026quot;:true,\u0026quot;134245418\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;134245529\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;201341983\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335551550\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335551620\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335557856\u0026quot;:4278190080,\u0026quot;335559685\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559731\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559737\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559738\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559739\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559740\u0026quot;:240}\"\u003e \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003ch3\u003e5. The Hornets\u0026#8217; Hum\u003c/h3\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan data-contrast=\"none\"\u003eIf there were an award for emerging out of nowhere, then the Charlotte Hornets would be a runaway winner. This team was dormant and stuck in the mud until right after the holidays. Suddenly the switch flipped and the Hornets took themselves out of the draft lottery conversation, and here they are, in the thick of play-in territory and rising.\u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan data-ccp-props=\"{\u0026quot;134245417\u0026quot;:true,\u0026quot;134245418\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;134245529\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;201341983\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335551550\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335551620\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335557856\u0026quot;:4278190080,\u0026quot;335559685\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559731\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559737\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559738\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559739\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559740\u0026quot;:240}\"\u003e \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp data-ccp-border-bottom=\"0px none #000000\" data-ccp-padding-bottom=\"0px\" data-ccp-border-between=\"0px none #000000\" data-ccp-padding-between=\"0px\"\u003e\u003cspan data-contrast=\"none\"\u003eThe surprise is that the Hornets have done this without a major acquisition at the trade deadline. Their development is strictly the result of in-house growth and obviously the immediate impact of Kon Knueppel, a favorite for Rookie of the Year and already one of the league’s best shooters.\u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan data-ccp-props=\"{\u0026quot;134245417\u0026quot;:true,\u0026quot;134245418\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;134245529\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;201341983\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335551550\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335551620\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335557856\u0026quot;:4278190080,\u0026quot;335559685\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559731\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559737\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559738\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559739\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559740\u0026quot;:240}\"\u003e \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp data-ccp-border-bottom=\"0px none #000000\" data-ccp-padding-bottom=\"0px\" data-ccp-border-between=\"0px none #000000\" data-ccp-padding-between=\"0px\"\u003e\u003cspan data-contrast=\"none\"\u003eLaMelo Ball is healthy and frisky. Charlotte has four players who can get 20 or more points a night — Ball, Knueppel, Brandon Miller, and Miles Bridges. The Hornets have won 10 of their last 11 with signature wins over the Spurs and Rockets. A seven-game home stand next month will tell; the Knicks, Sixers and Celtics (perhaps with Tatum) will pay a visit to an arena that’s suddenly swelling with noise.\u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan data-ccp-props=\"{\u0026quot;134245417\u0026quot;:true,\u0026quot;134245418\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;134245529\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;201341983\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335551550\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335551620\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335557856\u0026quot;:4278190080,\u0026quot;335559685\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559731\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559737\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559738\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559739\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559740\u0026quot;:240}\"\u003e \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003ch3 data-ccp-border-bottom=\"0px none #000000\" data-ccp-padding-bottom=\"0px\" data-ccp-border-between=\"0px none #000000\" data-ccp-padding-between=\"0px\"\u003e6. calf injury.\u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan He remains a premier talent, and if he blends with his new surroundings, the Bucks, winners of five of their last six games without Giannis, could be a team no one wants to see in the playoffs. MVP Race\u003c/h3\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan data-contrast=\"none\"\u003eThere\u0026#8217;s an interesting game on April 10, a final week tip-off that otherwise would seem relatively meaningless for two teams headed for the playoffs. Except: It’s OKC vs. Denver, meaning Shai vs. Jokić. Might the MVP race still be a toss-up at such a late stage?\u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan data-ccp-props=\"{\u0026quot;134245417\u0026quot;:true,\u0026quot;134245418\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;134245529\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;201341983\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335551550\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335551620\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335557856\u0026quot;:4278190080,\u0026quot;335559685\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559731\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559737\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559738\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559739\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559740\u0026quot;:240}\"\u003e \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp data-ccp-border-bottom=\"0px none #000000\" data-ccp-padding-bottom=\"0px\" data-ccp-border-between=\"0px none #000000\" data-ccp-padding-between=\"0px\"\u003e\u003cspan data-contrast=\"none\"\u003eShai and Jokić are front-runners at the moment. Others are in the mix as well, but this race appears to be a carbon copy of last season’s when Shai emerged as the winner, mainly because OKC ran away with the best record in the West. There’s no such cushion this time between the teams. Therefore, suspense awaits.\u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan data-ccp-props=\"{\u0026quot;134245417\u0026quot;:true,\u0026quot;134245418\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;134245529\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;201341983\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335551550\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335551620\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335557856\u0026quot;:4278190080,\u0026quot;335559685\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559731\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559737\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559738\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559739\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559740\u0026quot;:240}\"\u003e \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003ch3\u003e8. Rookie Race\u003c/h3\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan data-contrast=\"none\"\u003eHas Cooper Flagg wrapped up the Rookie of the Year award? It appears so. There’s an argument that Flagg should’ve been the first rookie since Blake Griffin to make the All-Star Game (he didn’t, despite averaging 20 points and six rebounds). That’s how impactful he is for the Dallas Mavericks, and why the Mavs have already taken steps (trading Anthony Davis) to begin the process of building around their new centerpiece.\u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan data-ccp-props=\"{\u0026quot;134245417\u0026quot;:true,\u0026quot;134245418\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;134245529\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;201341983\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335551550\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335551620\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335557856\u0026quot;:4278190080,\u0026quot;335559685\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559731\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559737\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559738\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559739\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559740\u0026quot;:240}\"\u003e \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp data-ccp-border-bottom=\"0px none #000000\" data-ccp-padding-bottom=\"0px\" data-ccp-border-between=\"0px none #000000\" data-ccp-padding-between=\"0px\"\u003e\u003cspan data-contrast=\"none\"\u003eIt will be Flagg or his Duke teammate, Knueppel, who’s averaging 19 points and shooting 43% from deep, taking honors. Actually, the only question is this: Why didn’t Duke win the national title with those two?\u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan data-ccp-props=\"{\u0026quot;134245417\u0026quot;:true,\u0026quot;134245418\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;134245529\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;201341983\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335551550\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335551620\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335557856\u0026quot;:4278190080,\u0026quot;335559685\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559731\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559737\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559738\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559739\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559740\u0026quot;:240}\"\u003e \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003ch3\u003e9. Key Games in the Final Two Weeks\u003c/h3\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan data-contrast=\"none\"\u003eSeveral teams will get pressed in the stretch run because they’ll either be boxing out for playoff seeding, trying to avoid the play-in tournament or trying to make the play-in cut.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp data-ccp-border-bottom=\"0px none #000000\" data-ccp-padding-bottom=\"0px\" data-ccp-border-between=\"0px none #000000\" data-ccp-padding-between=\"0px\"\u003e\u003cspan data-contrast=\"none\"\u003eBecause of that, there’s almost guaranteed to be drama right before the close of the regular season. Here are four games to circle:\u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan data-ccp-props=\"{\u0026quot;134245417\u0026quot;:true,\u0026quot;134245418\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;134245529\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;201341983\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335551550\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335551620\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335557856\u0026quot;:4278190080,\u0026quot;335559685\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559731\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559737\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559738\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559739\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559740\u0026quot;:240}\"\u003e \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cul\u003e\n\u003cli data-ccp-border-bottom=\"0px none #000000\" data-ccp-padding-bottom=\"0px\" data-ccp-border-between=\"0px none #000000\" data-ccp-padding-between=\"0px\"\u003e\u003cspan data-contrast=\"none\"\u003e\u003cstrong\u003ePistons at Thunder, Mar. 30:\u003c/strong\u003e Will these teams still hold a lead in their respective conferences? Being a top seed has its rewards.\u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan data-ccp-props=\"{\u0026quot;134245417\u0026quot;:true,\u0026quot;134245418\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;134245529\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;201341983\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335551550\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335551620\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335557856\u0026quot;:4278190080,\u0026quot;335559685\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559731\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559737\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559738\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559739\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559740\u0026quot;:240}\"\u003e \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli data-ccp-border-bottom=\"0px none #000000\" data-ccp-padding-bottom=\"0px\" data-ccp-border-between=\"0px none #000000\" data-ccp-padding-between=\"0px\"\u003e\u003cspan data-contrast=\"none\"\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eSpurs and Cavs at Warriors, April 1 and 2:\u003c/strong\u003e This back-to-back could serve as the last stand for Stephen Curry, this season anyway, as the Warriors try to make a playoff push. They’re currently in eighth place and seemingly locked in the play-in tournament at the moment.\u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan data-ccp-props=\"{\u0026quot;134245417\u0026quot;:true,\u0026quot;134245418\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;134245529\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;201341983\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335551550\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335551620\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335557856\u0026quot;:4278190080,\u0026quot;335559685\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559731\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559737\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559738\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559739\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559740\u0026quot;:240}\"\u003e \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli data-ccp-border-bottom=\"0px none #000000\" data-ccp-padding-bottom=\"0px\" data-ccp-border-between=\"0px none #000000\" data-ccp-padding-between=\"0px\"\u003e\u003cspan data-contrast=\"none\"\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eRockets at Suns, April 7:\u003c/strong\u003e Durant will see his old mates in a game that might drip with implications. Both Houston and Phoenix, along with others, are flirting with the sixth-place cutoff for guaranteed playoff spots; the Suns and Rockets are currently separated by two games in the West.\u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan data-ccp-props=\"{\u0026quot;134245417\u0026quot;:true,\u0026quot;134245418\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;134245529\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;201341983\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335551550\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335551620\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335557856\u0026quot;:4278190080,\u0026quot;335559685\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559731\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559737\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559738\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559739\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559740\u0026quot;:240}\"\u003e \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli data-ccp-border-bottom=\"0px none #000000\" data-ccp-padding-bottom=\"0px\" data-ccp-border-between=\"0px none #000000\" data-ccp-padding-between=\"0px\"\u003e\u003cspan data-contrast=\"none\"\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eCeltics at Knicks, April 9:\u003c/strong\u003e These rivals are currently ranked second and third in the East. Remember, the second seed has home court advantage until the conference finals, and maybe even then, should the top seed tumble.\u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan data-ccp-props=\"{\u0026quot;134245417\u0026quot;:true,\u0026quot;134245418\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;134245529\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;201341983\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335551550\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335551620\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335557856\u0026quot;:4278190080,\u0026quot;335559685\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559731\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559737\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559738\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559739\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559740\u0026quot;:240}\"\u003e \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003c/ul\u003e\n\u003ch3\u003e10. LeBron and the Lakers\u003c/h3\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan data-contrast=\"none\"\u003eIs the end approaching for a legend and a legendary franchise? LeBron James remains mum about his future plans; he can become a free agent this summer. Also, with a change in ownership, there has been no hint from the organization regarding where it stands on the subject.\u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan data-ccp-props=\"{\u0026quot;134245417\u0026quot;:true,\u0026quot;134245418\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;134245529\u0026quot;:false,\u0026quot;201341983\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335551550\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335551620\u0026quot;:1,\u0026quot;335557856\u0026quot;:4278190080,\u0026quot;335559685\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559731\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559737\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559738\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559739\u0026quot;:0,\u0026quot;335559740\u0026quot;:240}\"\u003e \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp data-ccp-border-bottom=\"0px none #000000\" data-ccp-padding-bottom=\"0px\" data-ccp-border-between=\"0px none #000000\" data-ccp-padding-between=\"0px\"\u003e\u003cspan data-contrast=\"none\"\u003eSo everything’s cloudy in LA. Also uncertain is the Lakers’ stretch run and how far it stretches. LeBron and Luka Dončić are All-Stars and the chemistry between the two seems fine. Austin Reaves is healthy again and impactful. But partly because of injuries, the Lakers haven’t given off contending vibes because defense and depth remain problematic.\u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan gold_text:
10 storylines to follow during stretch run of regular season
Here are 10 things that could happen, or not, in the final weeks of the season.
There’s often a tendency to label the post-All-Star break as the dog days of the regular season schedule, although this can also be misleading to a point. What if these dog days have bark? 

The season isn’t over, and in some respects, a segment of the season is just beginning. There’s still so much up for grabs, so much unsettled, so much at stake. And it’ll require a month or maybe more to resolve. 

The stretch run is where the championship contenders separate themselves from the pack and where the gap begins to expand between playoff hopefuls. Or maybe those gaps don’t expand; maybe they need an 82nd game. 

These are the 10 pressing issues and developments waiting to be unraveled between now and mid-April — if not beyond:

1. Jayson Tatum’s Decision
This is by far the most substantial stretch-run must-see because of its obvious, far-reaching implications. If Tatum announces a return to the Boston Celtics— and even more, displays little to no rust — it could disrupt the power structure of the East and the league as well. 

That’s how much Tatum, star of the 2024 championship run and All-NBA player out since injuring his Achilles last spring, means to the Celtics and how much a difference-maker of his magnitude can make in the playoffs. With a boost from Jaylen Brown, the Celtics are flourishing anyway without Tatum, currently at 35-19 and in second place. 

Tatum has begun light workouts in a clear indication of an attempted return. If that happens, it must take place no later than March; Tatum would require multiple tune-ups to prepare for the intensity of the playoffs. 

Brown said Tatum is “checking off all the right boxes,” and Kevin Durant, who successfully returned from the same injury five years ago, has high hopes for Tatum. 

“I expect to see All-Star level play and the same Jayson Tatum we’ve seen before he was injured,” Durant said. “Usually around this time, you’re playing a lot of five-on-five, playing against live bodies, just trying to get your rhythm and tempo back. So I’m looking forward to seeing him get out there with his team.” 

2. OKC at Full Strength
The 2025-26 season has been a unique one, to say the least, for the defending champion Thunder. They ripped through a 24-1 start and created comparisons with the 73-win Warriors. On the flip side, they’ve been spooked by the San Antonio Spurs, who have beaten OKC four times. 

Through it all, OKC has never been fully healthy, with Jalen Williams missing 30 games, Isaiah Hartenstein 16 straight from December through January and Shai Gilgeous-Alexander the last five. 

That’s about to change, and just in time. Williams is starting to regain his timing following off-season wrist surgery; Hartenstein is back from a calf strain and Shai’s abdominal injury was never projected to keep him from the lineup for a substantial stretch. 

With a complete rotation that features the reigning MVP, solid defense and stretchy depth that added Jared McCain at the trade deadline, can the Thunder finish the season the way they started it and ride that momentum into the playoffs? 

3. Denver Looking for Dominance
An off-season of change helped reshape the Nuggets and position them for another attempted takeover of the West, helped largely by three-time MVP Nikola Jokić. That process remains on hold, though, because the Nuggets are still trying to find their footing and traction. 

Jokić missed nearly a month after suffering a hyperextended knee, and the Nuggets’ rhythm has been off since his return; Denver is just 3-5 (although one loss was in overtime and two others were by a combined five points). While they’re getting positive play from Peyton Watson in an upgraded role, Aaron Gordon won’t be re-evaluated for another few weeks for his hamstring injury. 

So, the Nuggets aren’t whole just yet. However, there’s upside. They’re on the clock, though, because the West is loaded and the margin for error just shrank. 

4. The Cleveland Connection
Donovan Mitchell’s new backcourt running partner is James Harden and the honeymoon is blissful. The Cavs are undefeated in three games with Harden and are displaying a different dynamic. 

Harden is ninth on the all-time career scoring list yet seems more willing to rely on his passing skills, and perhaps justifiably so. Mitchell is a high-volume shooter and the other Cavs — Evan Mobley and Jarrett Allen in particular — require a creator. Harden is very good in that role; he worked wonders with Ivica Zubac with the Clippers, turning Zubac into a primary scoring option. Harden is averaging over nine assists with the Cavs. 

This represents a major philosophical change by a contender in the middle of the season. Usually, these hasty transitional situations either don’t work or require more time. In this case, Harden might just be what Mitchell and the Cavs ordered. 

5. The Hornets’ Hum
If there were an award for emerging out of nowhere, then the Charlotte Hornets would be a runaway winner. This team was dormant and stuck in the mud until right after the holidays. Suddenly the switch flipped and the Hornets took themselves out of the draft lottery conversation, and here they are, in the thick of play-in territory and rising. 

The surprise is that the Hornets have done this without a major acquisition at the trade deadline. Their development is strictly the result of in-house growth and obviously the immediate impact of Kon Knueppel, a favorite for Rookie of the Year and already one of the league’s best shooters. 

LaMelo Ball is healthy and frisky. Charlotte has four players who can get 20 or more points a night — Ball, Knueppel, Brandon Miller, and Miles Bridges. The Hornets have won 10 of their last 11 with signature wins over the Spurs and Rockets. A seven-game home stand next month will tell; the Knicks, Sixers and Celtics (perhaps with Tatum) will pay a visit to an arena that’s suddenly swelling with noise. 

6. The Bucks Try to Bounce Back
It wasn’t too long ago when the Bucks seemed on the verge of collapse. The season started poorly, Myles Turner’s impact was marginal at best, they lost nine of 10 and then Giannis Antetokounmpo gave off weird vibes leading up to the trade deadline and while tending to a calf injury. 

Giannis wasn’t swapped at the deadline and the best the Bucks could do to improve the roster was sift through the bargain bin and add some players on the cheap. 

Well, Cam Thomas and Ousmane Dieng seem like perfect fits for a team that needed scoring and an emerging young talent. Meanwhile, Ryan Rollins and Kevin Porter Jr. are still making the most of their upgraded roles in the rotation. 

Giannis is due back any day from his injury. He remains a premier talent, and if he blends with his new surroundings, the Bucks, winners of five of their last six games without Giannis, could be a team no one wants to see in the playoffs. Provided Milwaukee gets there, of course. 

7. MVP Race
There’s an interesting game on April 10, a final week tip-off that otherwise would seem relatively meaningless for two teams headed for the playoffs. Except: It’s OKC vs. Denver, meaning Shai vs. Jokić. Might the MVP race still be a toss-up at such a late stage? 

Shai and Jokić are front-runners at the moment. Others are in the mix as well, but this race appears to be a carbon copy of last season’s when Shai emerged as the winner, mainly because OKC ran away with the best record in the West. There’s no such cushion this time between the teams. Therefore, suspense awaits. 

8. Rookie Race
Has Cooper Flagg wrapped up the Rookie of the Year award? It appears so. There’s an argument that Flagg should’ve been the first rookie since Blake Griffin to make the All-Star Game (he didn’t, despite averaging 20 points and six rebounds). That’s how impactful he is for the Dallas Mavericks, and why the Mavs have already taken steps (trading Anthony Davis) to begin the process of building around their new centerpiece. 

It will be Flagg or his Duke teammate, Knueppel, who’s averaging 19 points and shooting 43% from deep, taking honors. Actually, the only question is this: Why didn’t Duke win the national title with those two? 

9. Key Games in the Final Two Weeks
Several teams will get pressed in the stretch run because they’ll either be boxing out for playoff seeding, trying to avoid the play-in tournament or trying to make the play-in cut.

Because of that, there’s almost guaranteed to be drama right before the close of the regular season. Here are four games to circle: 

Pistons at Thunder, Mar. 30: Will these teams still hold a lead in their respective conferences? Being a top seed has its rewards. 
Spurs and Cavs at Warriors, April 1 and 2: This back-to-back could serve as the last stand for Stephen Curry, this season anyway, as the Warriors try to make a playoff push. They’re currently in eighth place and seemingly locked in the play-in tournament at the moment. 
Rockets at Suns, April 7: Durant will see his old mates in a game that might drip with implications. Both Houston and Phoenix, along with others, are flirting with the sixth-place cutoff for guaranteed playoff spots; the Suns and Rockets are currently separated by two games in the West. 
Celtics at Knicks, April 9: These rivals are currently ranked second and third in the East. Remember, the second seed has home court advantage until the conference finals, and maybe even then, should the top seed tumble. 
10. LeBron and the Lakers
Is the end approaching for a legend and a legendary franchise? LeBron James remains mum about his future plans; he can become a free agent this summer. Also, with a change in ownership, there has been no hint from the organization regarding where it stands on the subject. 

So everything’s cloudy in LA. Also uncertain is the Lakers’ stretch run and how far it stretches. LeBron and Luka Dončić are All-Stars and the chemistry between the two seems fine. Austin Reaves is healthy again and impactful. But partly because of injuries, the Lakers haven’t given off contending vibes because defense and depth remain problematic. 

“When we’ve played some of our best basketball of the season, we’ve looked very good,” he said. “On the other side, when we’ve been terrible, we’ve looked disgusting.” 

Whenever the season ends in LA, only one question will beg for an answer: What next, LeBron? 

“I want to live,” he said over the weekend. “When I know, you guys will know. I don’t know. I have no idea. I just want to live. That’s all.” 

* * *

Shaun Powell has covered the NBA since 1985. You can e-mail him at spowell@nba.com, find his archive here and follow him on X.

data-drawer-open="false"><div id="__next"><div data-esi="true" style="display: none;"><div>
  <esi:vars>
    <esi:choose>
      <esi:when test="$(HTTP_USER_AGENT) has_i 'iphone'">
        <esi:assign name="isMobile">true</esi:assign>
        <esi:assign name="device">ios</esi:assign>
      </esi:when>
      <esi:when test="$(HTTP_USER_AGENT) has_i 'ipad'">
        <esi:assign name="isMobile">true</esi:assign>
        <esi:assign name="device">ios</esi:assign>
      </esi:when>
      <esi:when test="$(HTTP_USER_AGENT) has_i 'android'">
        <esi:assign name="isMobile">true</esi:assign>
        <esi:assign name="device">android</esi:assign>
      </esi:when>
      <esi:otherwise>
        <esi:assign name="isMobile">false</esi:assign>
        <esi:assign name="device">computer</esi:assign>
      </esi:otherwise>
    </esi:choose>

    <esi:choose>
      <esi:when test="$(QUERY_STRING{'bypassCountry'})">
        <esi:assign name="bpc">$upper($url_encode($(QUERY_STRING{'bypassCountry'})))</esi:assign>
      </esi:when>
      <esi:otherwise>
        <esi:assign name="bpc">NONE</esi:assign>
      </esi:otherwise>
    </esi:choose>

    <esi:choose>
      <esi:when test="$(bpc)==''">
        <esi:assign name="country">$url_encode($(GEO{'country_code'}))</esi:assign>
      </esi:when>
      <esi:when test="$(bpc)=='NONE'">
        <esi:assign name="country">$url_encode($(GEO{'country_code'}))</esi:assign>
      </esi:when>
      <esi:otherwise>
        <esi:assign name="country">$(bpc)</esi:assign>
      </esi:otherwise>
    </esi:choose>

    <esi:choose>
      <esi:when test="$(country)=='US'">
        <esi:assign name="isDomestic">true</esi:assign>
      </esi:when>
      <esi:otherwise>
        <esi:assign name="isDomestic">false</esi:assign>
      </esi:otherwise>
    </esi:choose>

    <script>
      var _esi = {
        country: "$(country)",
        tz: "$(GEO{'timezone'})",
        region: "$(GEO{'region_code'})",
        browser: "$url_encode($(HTTP_USER_AGENT{'browser'}))",
        os: "$url_encode($(HTTP_USER_AGENT{'os'}))",
        device: "$(device)",
        isMobile: "$(isMobile)",
        isDomestic: "$(isDomestic)",
        ip: "$(REMOTE_ADDR)"
      };
    </script>
  </esi:vars>

  <esi:vars>
    <esi:choose>
      <esi:when test="$(HTTP_USER_AGENT) has_i 'iphone'">
        <esi:assign name="isMobile">true</esi:assign>
        <esi:assign name="device">ios</esi:assign>
      </esi:when>
      <esi:when test="$(HTTP_USER_AGENT) has_i 'ipad'">
        <esi:assign name="isMobile">true</esi:assign>
        <esi:assign name="device">ios</esi:assign>
      </esi:when>
      <esi:when test="$(HTTP_USER_AGENT) has_i 'android'">
        <esi:assign name="isMobile">true</esi:assign>
        <esi:assign name="device">android</esi:assign>
      </esi:when>
      <esi:otherwise>
        <esi:assign name="isMobile">false</esi:assign>
        <esi:assign name="device">computer</esi:assign>
      </esi:otherwise>
    </esi:choose>

    <esi:choose>
      <esi:when test="$(QUERY_STRING{'bypassCountry'})">
        <esi:assign name="bpc">$upper($url_encode($(QUERY_STRING{'bypassCountry'})))</esi:assign>
      </esi:when>
      <esi:otherwise>
        <esi:assign name="bpc">NONE</esi:assign>
      </esi:otherwise>
    </esi:choose>

    <esi:choose>
      <esi:when test="$(bpc)==''">
        <esi:assign name="country">$url_encode($(GEO{'country_code'}))</esi:assign>
      </esi:when>
      <esi:when test="$(bpc)=='NONE'">
        <esi:assign name="country">$url_encode($(GEO{'country_code'}))</esi:assign>
      </esi:when>
      <esi:otherwise>
        <esi:assign name="country">$(bpc)</esi:assign>
      </esi:otherwise>
    </esi:choose>

    <esi:choose>
      <esi:when test="$(country)=='US'">
        <esi:assign name="isDomestic">true</esi:assign>
      </esi:when>
      <esi:otherwise>
        <esi:assign name="isDomestic">false</esi:assign>
      </esi:otherwise>
    </esi:choose>

</div></div><script id="gam-src" type="text/javascript" defer="" src="https://securepubads.g.doubleclick.net/tag/js/gpt.js"></script><script id="tealium-override" async="">
            window.utag_cfg_ovrd = window.utag_cfg_ovrd || {};
            window.utag_cfg_ovrd.noview = true;
          </script><script id="tealium-script" type="text/javascript" async="" src="https://tags.nba.com/nba-web/prod/utag.js"></script><script type="application/ld+json">{"@context":"https://schema.org/","@type":"Article","@id":"https://www.nba.com/news/starting-5-april-16-2026","headline":"Starting 5: Play-In thrills continue as Warriors, 76ers get wins","url":"https://www.nba.com/news/starting-5-april-16-2026","articleBody":"A golden comeback, capped by an instant-classic Steph Curry triple, in another Play-In thriller.\r\n\r\nAs coach Steve Kerr put it: 'This is why Steph came back.'\r\n\r\n\r\n\r\n\r\n\r\n5 STORIES IN TODAY'S EDITION 🏀\r\nDubs Stun Clips: Curry’s vintage flurry, Horford’s epic 4th and an unreal clutch rally\r\n\r\nWarriors' Way: With their season on the line, Golden State’s championship vets delivered\r\n\r\nPlayoff Philly: Key contributions and Maxey’s 31 points move Sixers past Magic\r\n\r\nFriday Look-Ahead: Magic-Hornets, Warriors-Suns, winner to Playoffs, loser goes home\r\n\r\nAwaiting Friday’s Winners: Shaun Powell examines what’s ahead for both No. 1 seeds\r\n\r\n\r\n\r\nBUT FIRST ... ⏰\r\n\r\n\r\nThe SoFi NBA Play-In Tournament sends its final two teams to the Playoffs – and two more home – on Friday, when the Magic host the Hornets (7:30 ET, Prime), followed by the Warriors visiting the Suns (10 ET, Prime).\r\n\r\nPlayoffs Saturday: Friday’s winners earn their conference’s No. 8 seed (but won't play until Sunday). The NBA Playoffs tip off Saturday with four Game 1s across Prime and ABC, followed by four more Sunday on ABC and NBC.\r\n\r\nViewership Jumps: 170 million people in the U.S. watched NBA games across ABC/ESPN, Amazon Prime Video, NBC/Peacock and NBA TV during the regular season – the most in 24 years and up 86% from last season.\r\n\r\n\r\n\r\n\r\n\r\n1. CURRY IGNITES WARRIORS PAST CLIPS IN ANOTHER PLAY-IN CLASSIC\r\n\r\n\r\nThey had lost seven of their last eight games entering Wednesday.\r\n\r\nThey were 8-33 this year when trailing entering the 4th quarter.\r\n\r\nBut with their season on the brink, staring at a 13-point deficit with 9:53 left in last night’s win-or-go-home showdown, the Warriors found a way.\r\n\r\nAnd in the biggest moment, it was Steph Curry.\r\n\r\nWarriors 126, Clippers 121: Curry (7 3s) dropped 27 of his 35 points in the 2nd half, willing Golden State back in the 4th alongside Al Horford (14 pts, 3 3s) as the Warriors closed on a 16-5 run, highlighted by Steph’s off-balance, go-ahead 3 with 50 seconds left to stun the Clippers. | Recap\r\n\r\n\r\n\r\nSteph Spark: After going down 10 early in the 2nd half, Curry caught fire for 16 points in the 3rd, drilling three triples in a three-minute span to cut the deficit to six entering the 4th.\r\n\r\nIt was only the start.\r\n\r\n \tClips Clap Back: After holding multiple double-digit leads, Bennedict Mathurin (23 pts) and Darius Garland (21 pts, 8 ast) pushed LA’s edge back to 13 with 9:53\r\n \tSteph Says Hello: Moments later, Curry checked back in and immediately splashed a 3, jumpstarting a push that trimmed the deficit to seven\r\n\r\nThen, Al’s Eruption: With the comeback brewing, Horford brought the jolt: a 3 to cut it to five. Another triple fifty seconds later. Then, a third — all within two minutes.\r\n\r\n \tClosing In: Curry and Gui Santos (20 pts) added two more buckets, trimming the deficit to one with 2:12 left\r\n \tThen, Horford Hit Again: It was his fourth 3 in 3:35 of game time – and the Warriors’ first lead since 8:11 in the 2nd quarter, up two with 2:12 left\r\n \tBut It Was Short Lived: LA quickly answered at the line, tying the game with under two to play\r\n\r\n\r\n\r\nThe Warriors needed one more bucket.\r\n\r\nSo they went to Steph.\r\n\r\nWith all five defenders keyed on him, Curry gave it up to Draymond Green at the top — then got it right back on a dribble handoff. He looked to turn the corner, but Brook Lopez walled him off, so he stepped back and fired:\r\n\r\n“Gooooooooood!”\r\n\r\nPrime’s Kevin Harlan was in disbelief. The Intuit Dome was in disarray. Curry was in the front row, watching it drop.\r\n\r\nWarriors up three with 50 seconds left.\r\n\r\nTwo steals from Green later, and Golden State was golden in the Play-In once again.\r\n\r\n\r\n\r\n2. WARRIORS' WAY: VETERAN CHAMPS LIFT GOLDEN STATE LATE\r\n\r\n\r\nWhen the lights turn up, Steph Curry shines brightest.\r\n\r\nExactly one year from last night, on April 15, 2025, Curry scored 15 of his 37 points in the 4th quarter of a Play-In game to beat Memphis, sending Golden State to the Playoffs.\r\n\r\nThat’s what the four-time NBA champion does. Tenth in all-time Playoff points and first in Playoff 3s, Curry’s built for moments like these. Late, tight, season on the line — where time and time again, Curry delivers.\r\n\r\nAnd he’s owned the SoFi Play-In stage.\r\n\r\n \tMeeting The Moment: Steph has played in five career Play-In games. He’s scored 35+ points in four of them\r\n \tAnother Level: Only one other player has multiple career 35+ point performances in the Play-In: Ja Morant, with two\r\n \tAlways Dangerous: Curry also owns the highest scoring average in Play-In Tournament history (min. 3 GP), with a scorching 34 ppg\r\n \tUnfazed Fire: That included playing past a minutes restriction last night as he works his way back from a knee injury, making multiple trips to the locker room before taking over\r\n \tThat Takeover? Seven 3s on 12-of-23 shooting, including a 10-of-14 mark in the 2nd half – capped by another clutch Curry banger\r\n \t“All it takes is one,” said Steve Kerr postgame of Curry’s outbursts. “That’s his magic. He can will himself into a game. He has so much belief, so much competitive desire – it’s amazing to watch.”\r\n\r\n\r\n\r\nThat competitive desire fuels a veteran-laden Warriors squad with championship DNA – and it showed up Wednesday.\r\n\r\nIt started with Al Horford.\r\n\r\n \tWorth The Wait: Horford, who won the 2024 NBA title with Boston, has battled through injuries in his first season with Golden State, missing 37 games\r\n \tBut he saved his biggest game for the biggest spot, scoring 12 in the 4th with his four late 3s – matching his highest-scoring quarter of the season\r\n\r\nThe night ended with Draymond Green.\r\n\r\n \tWinning Hustle: After Curry’s go-ahead 3, Green stole LA’s inbound to set up a Brandin Podziemski bucket\r\n \t10 Seconds Later: “Green stole the ball!” Another swipe, this time on Kawhi Leonard (21 pts, 7 ast), who Green limited to 2 points in the 4th\r\n \tWinning Duo: Draymond finished with 7 points, 6 boards, 9 assists and a game-high 4 steals, as he and Curry reached the Playoffs together for the 11th time\r\n \t“I’ve been watching them for 12 years,” said Kerr of Curry and Green. “There’s a reason we have four championships, and it’s the competitiveness. The heart. The will…\r\n \t“It's been such a tough year, but to just show what they're made of – what we're made of … with all the wins we’ve ever had here … this one is right up there.”\r\n\r\nUp Next? Another elimination game, as the Warriors visit the Suns on Friday (10 ET) to decide the West’s No. 8 seed in the Playoffs. The loser goes home.\r\n\r\n\r\n\r\n3. SIXERS CLAIM NO. 7 SEED: MAXEY MOVES PHILLY PAST ORLANDO\r\n\r\n\r\nA year with its share of key injuries and absences.\r\n\r\nAn emergency appendectomy for franchise star Joel Embiid.\r\n\r\nA Play-In elimination setting promising the unpredictable.\r\n\r\nQuestions continued to follow the 76ers and their 2025-26 aspirations through the finish of the regular season.\r\n\r\nWednesday night, Tyrese Maxey had an answer.\r\n\r\nIn front of Allen Iverson and the Philly faithful and in the classic black jerseys the team wore in the 2001 Finals, Maxey drove the Sixers back to the Playoffs after missing out last year.\r\n\r\nSixers 109, Magic 97: Maxey scored 11 of his 31 points in the 4th quarter, including a stretch of 7 straight that pulled Philly out of a one-possession game to a double-digit win, claiming the No. 7 seed for the Sixers and sending them to a First-Round series in Boston.\r\n\r\nDesmond Bane’s game-high 34 points led the Magic, who now return home to host the 9-seed Hornets in the SoFi Play-In Tournament finale Friday to decide the 8-seed. | Recap\r\n\r\n\r\n\r\n \t“I told [the team] at the beginning of the year, ‘I don’t care what it takes, we’re going to the Playoffs,’” Maxey said postgame\r\n \tWhat It Took: Tyrese got his 31 efficiently but on a game-high 25 shots (44 FG%), leading all players with 42 minutes, following a season where he played the most minutes of his career (38 mpg)\r\n \tMaxey’s Move: After a 24-14 Magic run made it 86-87 with under 8 minutes to go, Tyrese took off with a floater, a triple and another floater to stretch the lead back to 8\r\n \tThe Close: Orlando never got back within 4, with Maxey rising up for a game-sealing block in the final minute, before Andre Drummond (14 pts, 10 reb) delivered the dagger 3\r\n\r\n“He’s been doing a bit of that lately… understanding we need his greatness at the right time,” coach Nick Nurse said of Maxey and his decisive 7-point burst.\r\n\r\n \tTop-5 Ty: Maxey’s 31 is good for the 5th-highest scoring performance of this year’s Play-In Tournament, after ranking top-5 in scoring (career-best 28.3 ppg) throughout a season in which all his offensive averages increased\r\n \tWinning Wings: Joining Maxey with over 40 minutes played Wednesday, VJ Edgecombe (11 reb) and Kelly Oubre Jr. (5 3s) contributed 19 points apiece\r\n \tWhat’s Next: The 7-seed Sixers now face the 2-seed Celtics Sunday, the start of an NBA-record 23rd Playoff series between the two teams. No other postseason matchup has met more than 15 times\r\n\r\n\r\n\r\nThe Magic still have another chance to join the Sixers in that Playoff field, facing a Hornets squad that’s won its way into the Play-In finale, and six of its last eight games.\r\n\r\n \t“There’s frustration, but it’s also a great opportunity ahead of us on Friday,” Bane said. “Get the chance to play at home in front of our fans… and the chance to go clinch a Playoff spot. I’m excited for the challenge.”\r\n \tOrlando’s Outputs: After Bane’s 34 on Wednesday, the Magic got a combined 43 points from Paolo Banchero (18), Anthony Black (13) and Franz Wagner (12)\r\n\r\n\r\n\r\n\r\n4. FRIDAY LOOK-AHEAD: WIN OR GO HOME ON PRIME\r\n\r\n\r\nAfter two nights of 7 vs. 8 and 9 vs. 10 Play-In games, the stakes take another step up in Friday’s Play-In finale.\r\n\r\nWin? A date with the No. 1 seed in the NBA Playoffs.\r\n\r\nLose? That’s a wrap on 2025-26.\r\n\r\nWe’ll dive more into each matchup in Friday’s edition, but let’s get you started here…\r\n\r\n\r\n\r\nEast | No. 10 Hornets at No. 8 Magic (7:30 ET, Prime): LaMelo Ball, Brandon Miller and Kon Knueppel lead Charlotte against Paolo Banchero, Desmond Bane and Franz Wagner in a battle of opposing styles.\r\n\r\n \t3s Are Key: While Charlotte led the regular season in made 3s (16.4), no team allowed fewer triples than Orlando, which held its opponents to just 12.1 per game\r\n \tLate Magic: Although the Magic couldn’t edge Philly last night, they’ve shined in crunch time this season with 21 clutch wins – tied with the Pistons for the most in the NBA\r\n \tBuzzing Hornets: They’ll now face a Hornets squad coming off an electric OT win over Miami in Tuesday’s winner-take-all duel – moving them one win shy of their first Playoff berth in a decade\r\n \tUp Next: The winner earns the East’s No. 8 seed, setting up a First Round matchup against the No. 1 Pistons (Game 1: Sunday at 6:30 ET, NBC)\r\n\r\n\r\n\r\nWest | No. 10 Warriors at No. 7 Suns (10 ET, Prime): Steph, Draymond & Co. look to complete their climb against Devin Booker, Jalen Green and Dillon Brooks.\r\n\r\n \tSeries Rewind: Golden State took three of four from Phoenix this season, with the Suns earning a last-minute 99-98 win Dec. 18 in which they held Curry to 2-of-9 from 3\r\n \tThree & D Edge: Phoenix (45-37) finished 8 games ahead of Golden State and joined Boston as the only teams to rank top-5 in 3s (14.8 3pg) and top-10 in defense (112.9)\r\n \tMore History? The Dubs aim for a second road win to join last year’s Miami Heat as the only 10-seeds to advance out of the SoFi Play-In Tournament\r\n \tOKC Awaits: The West’s No. 8 seed goes to the winner, sending them into the First Round against the defending-champion 1-seed Thunder (Game 1: Sunday at 3:30 ET, ABC)\r\n\r\n\r\n\r\n\r\n5. AWAITING FRIDAY’S WINNERS: WHAT LIES AHEAD FOR THE NO. 1 SEEDS\r\n\r\n\r\nAsk the last three NBA champions:\r\n\r\n2025: No. 1-seed Thunder.\r\n\r\n2024: No. 1-seed Celtics.\r\n\r\n2023: No. 1-seed Nuggets.\r\n\r\nThe top seed in the Playoffs is a coveted spot to be in. But what are some of the challenges?\r\n\r\nThis year’s Thunder and Pistons, for instance, won’t have their First Round Playoff matchups solidified until Friday’s Play-In finale -- making them the last seeds to have their opponent set.\r\n\r\nNBA.com’s Shaun Powell examines the potential traps and hurdles OKC and Detroit could face on the Playoff road ahead, as they prepare for their Play-In challengers:\r\n\r\nBeing a top seed in the NBA playoffs has its privileges. Home-court advantage throughout the rounds and the most favorable matchups every round from a seeding standpoint are the obvious two…\r\n\r\n[But] in the Playoffs, even with a seven-game series, sometimes the hotter team ultimately prevails, making all that hard work in the regular season moot. \r\n\r\nThese are the realities for the top-seeded Detroit Pistons and Oklahoma City Thunder starting next weekend.” | Read More\r\n\r\nWant to share Starting 5 with a friend? Send them this link.\r\n\r\nShape the Starting 5.  Email us here.\r\n\r\nDon't have the NBA App? Download it here.","dateCreated":"2026-04-16T13:01:25Z","description":"Stephen Curry erupts to stun Clippers, Tyrese Maxey and Sixers punch Playoff ticket and Friday’s Play-In finale is
